questions.question: How many steps does Tom climb to reach the top of the lighthouse?
options: One hundred and seven
One hundred and forty-seven
One hundred and fifty-seven
One hundred and thirty-seven
One hundred and forty
questions.correctAnswer: One hundred and forty-seven
questions.explanation: The passage states Tom climbed 'the one hundred and forty-seven steps to the top'. This is a retrieval question — the answer is directly stated in the text. ✓

questions.question: What colour is Barnacle the cat?
options: Black
Ginger and white
Marmalade (orange)
Grey
Tortoiseshell
questions.correctAnswer: Marmalade (orange)
questions.explanation: The passage describes Barnacle as 'a marmalade cat', which means he is orange-coloured. Marmalade cats are named after the orange preserve. ✓

questions.question: What does the phrase 'made his blood run cold' suggest Tom felt?
options: He was freezing from the fog
He was terrified by how close the ship was
He was angry at the ship's captain
He felt ill from climbing the stairs
He was bored of hearing horns
questions.correctAnswer: He was terrified by how close the ship was
questions.explanation: The phrase 'made his blood run cold' is an idiom meaning sudden fear or terror. Tom was frightened because the ship's horn was 'far too close to the rocks', meaning it was in danger of crashing. ✓

questions.question: Why did Barnacle's fur stand on end?
options: He was cold from the fog
He was reacting to the loud fog horn and the tense situation
He was excited to see the ship
He had been startled by Tom sitting down
He wanted to go outside
questions.correctAnswer: He was reacting to the loud fog horn and the tense situation
questions.explanation: A cat's fur stands on end when it is frightened or alarmed. Barnacle was reacting to the stressful situation — the loud fog horn blasts and Tom's urgent actions showed something was wrong. ✓

questions.question: What does the word 'companion' suggest about the relationship between Tom and Barnacle?
options: Barnacle is Tom's pet that he feeds
Barnacle is a stray cat who visits sometimes
Barnacle is a true friend and partner to Tom
Barnacle is a working cat who catches mice
Barnacle belongs to someone else
questions.correctAnswer: Barnacle is a true friend and partner to Tom
questions.explanation: The word 'companion' means more than just a pet — it suggests a close friend who shares your life and keeps you company. Tom's words ('better assistant than any human') and the way Barnacle follows him everywhere confirm this deep bond. ✓

questions.question: How does the author build tension in the fog scene?
options: By describing the beautiful sunset over the sea
By using short sentences, urgent actions, and frightening sounds
By telling us about Tom's daily routine climbing the stairs
By explaining how the lighthouse lamp works
By describing Barnacle's appearance in detail
questions.correctAnswer: By using short sentences, urgent actions, and frightening sounds
questions.explanation: The author builds tension through short, punchy sentences ('He pulled again and again'), urgent physical actions (grabbing the lever, pulling with all his strength), frightening sounds (ship's horn 'far too close'), and sensory details (barely seeing his hand, blood running cold). These techniques create a sense of danger and urgency. ✓

questions.question: On which days is the museum open?
options: Mondays and Fridays
Tuesdays and Saturdays
Wednesdays and Sundays
Every day except Monday
Saturdays only
questions.correctAnswer: Tuesdays and Saturdays
questions.explanation: The sign on the museum reads 'Open Tuesdays and Saturdays, 10am–4pm'. This information is stated directly in the passage. ✓

questions.question: Why did Priya decide to go into the museum on this particular day?
options: She had a school project about museums
It was raining and she had nothing else to do
Her parents told her to go
Mrs Greenwood invited her in
She wanted to buy something from the shop next door
questions.correctAnswer: It was raining and she had nothing else to do
questions.explanation: The passage says 'It was a rainy Saturday in March, and Priya had nothing else to do.' The bad weather and boredom combined to make her finally go inside after passing it many times. ✓

questions.question: What does the phrase 'a shiver of wonder' tell us about how Priya feels?
options: She is cold from the rain outside
She is frightened by the dinosaur tooth
She feels amazed and excited by the ancient object
She is bored and wants to leave
She is nervous about talking to Mrs Greenwood
questions.correctAnswer: She feels amazed and excited by the ancient object
questions.explanation: A 'shiver of wonder' describes a physical reaction caused by amazement. Priya is thrilled by the idea that something 140 million years old is sitting in her ordinary town. The word 'wonder' tells us she feels awe, not fear or cold. ✓

questions.question: What is the main theme or message of this passage?
options: Museums are boring places that nobody visits
Extraordinary things can be found in ordinary places
Children should spend less time on the internet
Dinosaurs once lived in Dorset
Old people are lonely and need visitors
questions.correctAnswer: Extraordinary things can be found in ordinary places
questions.explanation: The passage contrasts the ordinary setting (a small museum between a chip shop and post office, in an 'ordinary town') with the extraordinary objects inside (a 140-million-year-old tooth, a magical compass). The repetition of 'ordinary' at the end emphasises that amazing things can be found in unexpected places. ✓

questions.question: How many hedgehogs were there in Britain in the 1990s?
options: One million
Ten million
Thirty million
Fifty million
One hundred million
questions.correctAnswer: Thirty million
questions.explanation: The passage states 'In the 1990s, there were an estimated thirty million hedgehogs'. This is a retrieval question — the number is stated directly. ✓

questions.question: Why are hedgehogs particularly at risk from traffic?
options: They are too slow to cross roads
They curl into a ball instead of running away
They are attracted to car headlights
They like to sleep on warm roads
They cannot hear cars approaching
questions.correctAnswer: They curl into a ball instead of running away
questions.explanation: The passage explains that 'hedgehogs curl into a ball when frightened rather than running away', which makes them 'especially vulnerable to traffic'. Their natural defence mechanism doesn't work against cars. ✓

questions.question: What is a 'Hedgehog Highway'?
options: A special road built just for hedgehogs
A network of holes cut in garden fences so hedgehogs can travel between gardens
A charity that rescues injured hedgehogs
A path through the countryside where hedgehogs live
A tunnel under a busy road
questions.correctAnswer: A network of holes cut in garden fences so hedgehogs can travel between gardens
questions.explanation: The passage explains that the 'Hedgehog Highway' campaign encourages homeowners to 'cut small holes (about 13cm square) in the bottom of their garden fences, creating corridors that allow hedgehogs to move freely between gardens'. ✓

questions.question: What is the overall purpose of this passage?
options: To entertain readers with a funny story about hedgehogs
To explain why hedgehogs are declining and what people can do to help
To persuade people to stop driving cars
To describe what hedgehogs eat
To compare hedgehogs with other British wildlife
questions.correctAnswer: To explain why hedgehogs are declining and what people can do to help
questions.explanation: The passage has a clear informative and persuasive structure: it presents the problem (hedgehog decline), explains the causes (habitat loss, roads), and offers solutions (Hedgehog Highways, garden changes). Its purpose is to inform readers about the crisis and inspire action. ✓

questions.question: What temperature is the natural hot spring water?
options: 36 degrees Celsius
40 degrees Celsius
46 degrees Celsius
56 degrees Celsius
26 degrees Celsius
questions.correctAnswer: 46 degrees Celsius
questions.explanation: The passage states the water 'bubbled up from deep underground at a temperature of forty-six degrees Celsius'. ✓

questions.question: What was the Roman name for the town of Bath?
options: Sulis Minerva
Aquae Sulis
Caldarium
Londinium
Tepidarium
questions.correctAnswer: Aquae Sulis
questions.explanation: The passage says 'The town that grew around it became known as Aquae Sulis, meaning the waters of Sulis.' ✓

questions.question: What does the passage suggest about the role of the baths in Roman daily life?
options: They were only used for washing
They were the centre of social, business, and leisure activity
They were reserved for wealthy Romans only
They were mainly used for religious worship
They were only open on special occasions
questions.correctAnswer: They were the centre of social, business, and leisure activity
questions.explanation: The passage says the baths were 'the heart of social life' where Romans would 'exercise, eat snacks, gossip, and conduct business'. This shows they were much more than just washing facilities. ✓

questions.question: What is a 'strigil'?
options: A type of Roman bath towel
A curved metal tool for scraping oil and dirt from skin
A channel used to carry hot water
A type of Roman exercise equipment
A stone used to heat the water
questions.correctAnswer: A curved metal tool for scraping oil and dirt from skin
questions.explanation: The passage explains that 'slaves would scrape oil and dirt from bathers' skin using curved metal tools called strigils'. ✓

questions.question: Why does the author describe the vaulted ceiling as 'a feat of architecture that would not be matched in Britain for over a thousand years'?
options: To show that the ceiling was very decorative
To emphasise how advanced Roman engineering was compared to what came after
To explain why the ceiling eventually collapsed
To compare Roman buildings with modern ones
To suggest the Romans wasted money on unnecessary building
questions.correctAnswer: To emphasise how advanced Roman engineering was compared to what came after
questions.explanation: By saying no one in Britain matched this engineering for over a thousand years, the author emphasises how remarkably advanced the Romans were. After they left, the skills and knowledge to build such structures were lost for centuries. ✓

questions.question: In what order did Roman bathers move through the rooms?
options: Cold, warm, hot
Hot, warm, cold
Warm, hot, cold
Cold, hot, warm
Hot, cold, warm
questions.correctAnswer: Warm, hot, cold
questions.explanation: The passage states they 'start in the tepidarium (warm room), then move to the caldarium (hot room)... and finally plunge into the frigidarium (cold pool)'. Warm → hot → cold. ✓

questions.question: How is the passage organised?
options: By comparing different Roman baths across Britain
Chronologically — from the Romans' arrival, through the baths' heyday, decline, and rediscovery
By listing arguments for and against visiting the baths
By describing one visitor's experience at the baths
Alphabetically by topic
questions.correctAnswer: Chronologically — from the Romans' arrival, through the baths' heyday, decline, and rediscovery
questions.explanation: The passage follows a clear timeline: the Romans arrive and build the baths, we learn what they were like at their peak, then the Romans leave and the baths fall to ruin, and finally they are rediscovered in the 18th century. This is chronological organisation. ✓

questions.question: Where do Kai and Isla set out from?
options: Studland Bay
Chapman's Pool
Old Harry Rocks
Poole Harbour
The Purbeck coast
questions.correctAnswer: Poole Harbour
questions.explanation: The passage states they 'set out from Poole Harbour at nine o'clock'. ✓

questions.question: What is Isla's role during the storm?
options: She controls the jib sheet
She steers the boat using the tiller
She shouts instructions to Kai
She looks out for the harbour
She bails water out of the dinghy
questions.correctAnswer: She steers the boat using the tiller
questions.explanation: Dave tells Isla to 'take the tiller and keep us pointing north-east'. The tiller is the handle used to steer a boat. ✓

questions.question: What does the simile 'The sky turned the colour of a bruise' suggest?
options: The sky was bright and cheerful
The sky looked dark, threatening, and unnatural
The sky was blue like the sea
The sky had beautiful sunset colours
The sky was completely black
questions.correctAnswer: The sky looked dark, threatening, and unnatural
questions.explanation: A bruise is dark purple/yellow and associated with injury and pain. Comparing the sky to a bruise creates an image of something threatening and unpleasant, warning of the dangerous storm to come. ✓

questions.question: How does the author contrast the beginning and middle of the passage?
options: By using different characters in each section
By changing from calm, beautiful imagery to violent, threatening imagery
By switching from Kai's viewpoint to Isla's
By moving from dialogue to description
By changing the setting from sea to land
questions.correctAnswer: By changing from calm, beautiful imagery to violent, threatening imagery
questions.explanation: The beginning uses peaceful language ('flat as a mirror', 'gentle breeze', 'gleaming') while the storm section uses violent language ('hit them like a slap', 'exploded', 'steep grey walls'). This stark contrast makes the danger feel more dramatic. ✓

questions.question: What does Isla's final comment — 'Same time tomorrow?' — reveal about her character?
options: She is forgetful and has already forgotten the danger
She is brave, resilient, and has a sense of humour despite the ordeal
She is being sarcastic and never wants to sail again
She is trying to impress Dave the instructor
She is angry and wants to prove the storm didn't scare her
questions.correctAnswer: She is brave, resilient, and has a sense of humour despite the ordeal
questions.explanation: After a genuinely frightening experience, Isla's joking comment shows courage and resilience — she can laugh about the danger rather than being defeated by it. The 'breathless, relief-soaked laughter' tells us she was scared but has bounced back quickly. ✓

questions.question: What is the main theme of this passage?
options: The dangers of sailing in bad weather
How teamwork and trust can help people overcome frightening situations
Why children should always listen to adults
The beauty of the Dorset coastline
How to sail a dinghy properly
questions.correctAnswer: How teamwork and trust can help people overcome frightening situations
questions.explanation: While the passage includes sailing and danger, its heart is about teamwork: Dave stays calm and gives clear instructions, Kai and Isla each do their jobs, and 'they worked as a team, each trusting the others completely'. The successful outcome comes from cooperation under pressure. ✓

questions.question: Where has the spider built her web?
options: In a tree
Between two gateposts
Inside a house
Across a window
Under a bridge
questions.correctAnswer: Between two gateposts
questions.explanation: The first line tells us the web is 'Between the gateposts, strung with care'. ✓

questions.question: What destroys the spider's web?
options: The wind blows it away
Rain washes it down
A person accidentally brushes against it
A bird flies through it
The sun dries it out
questions.correctAnswer: A person accidentally brushes against it
questions.explanation: The poem says 'careless boots come stamping past, / A sleeve brushes' — a person walking past carelessly brushes the web with their sleeve and destroys it. ✓

questions.question: The poet compares the spider's web to a 'palace'. What is this technique called?
options: Simile
Metaphor
Alliteration
Onomatopoeia
Rhyme
questions.correctAnswer: Metaphor
questions.explanation: The web is called 'a palace' directly — not 'like a palace'. When something IS described as something else (without using 'like' or 'as'), it is a metaphor. The extended metaphor continues with 'throne', 'banquet hall', and 'queen'. ✓

questions.question: What does 'The dewdrops hang like chandeliers' mean?
options: The dewdrops are made of glass
The dewdrops are hanging from the ceiling
The dewdrops look like sparkling, hanging light fixtures
The dewdrops are very heavy
The dewdrops are making a noise
questions.correctAnswer: The dewdrops look like sparkling, hanging light fixtures
questions.explanation: A chandelier is a decorative hanging light with crystal drops that sparkle. The simile compares the dewdrops on the web to chandeliers because they hang from the threads and glitter in the sunlight. ✓

questions.question: What qualities does the poet give the spider by calling her a 'patient queen'?
options: She is lazy and does nothing all day
She is dignified, calm, and in control of her territory
She is frightening and dangerous
She is sad and lonely
She is busy and rushing around
questions.correctAnswer: She is dignified, calm, and in control of her territory
questions.explanation: By calling the spider a 'patient queen', the poet uses personification to give her human qualities of dignity, calmness, and authority. A queen rules her domain with composure — the spider similarly presides over her web. ✓

questions.question: What message about life does the final stanza convey?
options: It is wrong to destroy nature
Spiders are the most hardworking creatures
Resilience and determination matter more than setbacks
Building things is a waste of time if they get destroyed
Queens should never show emotion
questions.correctAnswer: Resilience and determination matter more than setbacks
questions.explanation: The final stanza shows the spider rebuilding without complaint after her work is destroyed. 'She never cries. / She simply starts her work once more' conveys a powerful message about resilience — that setbacks don't have to defeat you if you have the determination to start again. ✓

questions.question: How many spines does a hedgehog have covering its back?
options: Around one thousand
Around five thousand
Around ten thousand
Around three thousand
Around fifty thousand
questions.correctAnswer: Around five thousand
questions.explanation: The passage states that hedgehogs have 'around five thousand spines covering its back.' This information is directly stated in the first paragraph. ✓

questions.question: What is the name given to a hedgehog's nest?
options: A burrow
A hibernacula
A warren
A lodge
A den
questions.correctAnswer: A hibernacula
questions.explanation: The passage clearly states that hedgehogs 'sleep in nests called hibernacula, which they build from leaves, grass and twigs.' This is directly stated in the second paragraph. ✓

questions.question: Why is it important for hedgehogs to eat as much as possible during summer and autumn?
options: Because food is more expensive in winter
Because they need to build up fat reserves to survive hibernation
Because they are growing their spines during that time
Because they are feeding their babies
Because the insects taste better in warm weather
questions.correctAnswer: Because they need to build up fat reserves to survive hibernation
questions.explanation: The passage says hedgehogs 'survive entirely on fat reserves built up during the summer and autumn' during their hibernation from November to March. This means they must eat a great deal before winter because they will not eat again until spring. The reader must connect these two pieces of information to reach this conclusion. ✓

questions.question: What does the word 'declined' mean in the phrase 'hedgehog numbers in Britain have declined sharply'?
options: Improved
Decreased
Remained the same
Spread out
Disappeared completely
questions.correctAnswer: Decreased
questions.explanation: In this context, 'declined' means decreased or fallen in number. The passage confirms this meaning by going on to say the population 'has fallen from approximately thirty million... to fewer than one million,' which shows the numbers have gone down. ✓

questions.question: Why does the author include specific population numbers from the 1950s and today?
options: To show that hedgehogs have always been rare
To prove that scientists are studying hedgehogs
To emphasise how dramatically hedgehog numbers have fallen
To encourage people to count hedgehogs in their gardens
To compare British hedgehogs with those in other countries
questions.correctAnswer: To emphasise how dramatically hedgehog numbers have fallen
questions.explanation: The author includes the specific figures — thirty million in the 1950s compared to fewer than one million today — to emphasise the scale of the decline. By giving the reader concrete numbers, the author makes the problem feel urgent and real, which supports the final paragraph's call to action for readers to help protect hedgehogs. ✓

questions.question: How does the author use contrast in the opening paragraph to engage the reader?
options: By comparing hedgehogs to other animals like foxes and badgers
By describing the hedgehog as looking fierce but actually being gentle
By contrasting daytime and night-time activities
By comparing British hedgehogs with hedgehogs in other countries
By showing the difference between wild and pet hedgehogs
questions.correctAnswer: By describing the hedgehog as looking fierce but actually being gentle
questions.explanation: The author creates contrast by saying the hedgehog 'may look fierce, but it is actually one of the gentlest creatures you could hope to meet.' This contrast between the prickly, fierce appearance and the gentle nature surprises the reader and immediately makes them curious to learn more about the animal. It is an effective technique for drawing the reader in. ✓

questions.question: What three items does the passage suggest young explorers should bring when visiting rock pools?
options: A towel, sunscreen and a hat
A camera, binoculars and a notebook
A bucket, a small net and a magnifying glass
Wellington boots, a torch and a bag
A fishing rod, a jar and a spade
questions.correctAnswer: A bucket, a small net and a magnifying glass
questions.explanation: The passage directly states that 'Armed with a bucket, a small net and a magnifying glass, young explorers can discover creatures they might never otherwise see.' These three items are clearly listed in the second paragraph. ✓

questions.question: What do beadlet anemones look like when the tide is out?
options: Beautiful flowers with waving tentacles
Bright green frilly clusters
Small starfish with five arms
Blobs of dark red jelly stuck to the rock
Tiny shrimps hiding under seaweed
questions.correctAnswer: Blobs of dark red jelly stuck to the rock
questions.explanation: The passage states that beadlet anemones 'look like blobs of dark red jelly stuck to the rock when the tide is out.' This description is directly given in the fourth paragraph. ✓

questions.question: Why do the tiny shrimps 'dart away at the slightest shadow'?
options: Because they are playing a game with each other
Because they are trying to find food
Because they do not like the light
Because they mistake shadows for predators and are trying to escape danger
Because they are swimming towards the open sea
questions.correctAnswer: Because they mistake shadows for predators and are trying to escape danger
questions.explanation: The passage does not directly explain why the shrimps dart away, but the reader can infer that darting away at the slightest shadow is a survival instinct. A shadow passing over a rock pool could be a bird or other predator, so the shrimps react quickly to avoid being eaten. This is a natural defensive behaviour. ✓

questions.question: What does the word 'teeming' mean in the phrase 'teeming with life'?
options: Empty of
Lacking in
Overflowing with
Struggling with
Decorated with
questions.correctAnswer: Overflowing with
questions.explanation: In this context, 'teeming' means overflowing with or full of. The passage describes the rock pools as being 'teeming with life,' and then goes on to describe many different creatures living there — limpets, periwinkles, shrimps, hermit crabs, anemones and starfish — which confirms that the pools are bursting with living things. ✓

questions.question: Why does the author describe hermit crabs as 'comical creatures' that live in 'borrowed shells'?
options: To warn readers that hermit crabs are dangerous
To suggest that hermit crabs are dishonest
To make the reader feel entertained and curious about their unusual behaviour
To explain why hermit crabs are endangered
To show that hermit crabs are not real crabs
questions.correctAnswer: To make the reader feel entertained and curious about their unusual behaviour
questions.explanation: The author uses the words 'comical' and 'borrowed' to create a humorous, entertaining tone that makes the reader smile and want to learn more. Describing the shells as 'borrowed' gives the hermit crabs an almost human quality, as if they are borrowing clothes, which adds charm and encourages the reader's interest in these quirky creatures. ✓

questions.question: The author calls rock pools 'miniature worlds' in the opening paragraph. What effect does this metaphor have?
options: It suggests that rock pools are too small to be interesting
It makes the reader think of outer space
It creates a sense of wonder by suggesting each pool is a complete, self-contained environment to explore
It shows that rock pools are exactly like the wider ocean
It implies that only tiny people could live there
questions.correctAnswer: It creates a sense of wonder by suggesting each pool is a complete, self-contained environment to explore
questions.explanation: By calling rock pools 'miniature worlds,' the author uses a metaphor that suggests each pool is like a tiny, complete universe full of life and activity. This creates a sense of wonder and adventure, making the reader feel as though peering into a rock pool is like discovering a hidden world. It elevates something small into something exciting and worth exploring. ✓

questions.question: How many bees can a single hive contain?
options: Up to six thousand
Up to sixteen thousand
Up to sixty thousand
Up to six hundred thousand
Up to six hundred
questions.correctAnswer: Up to sixty thousand
questions.explanation: The passage clearly states that 'A single hive can contain up to sixty thousand bees.' This information is directly given in the opening paragraph. ✓

questions.question: What is the name of the process by which a forager bee passes nectar to a house bee?
options: Pollination
Hibernation
Trophallaxis
Metamorphosis
Fermentation
questions.correctAnswer: Trophallaxis
questions.explanation: The passage states that 'the forager passes the nectar to a house bee through a process called trophallaxis, which is essentially mouth-to-mouth transfer.' The term is directly named in the fourth paragraph. ✓

questions.question: Why do you think the author describes the forager role as the one a worker bee 'finally graduates to'?
options: Because foragers earn more honey than other bees
Because it is the easiest job in the hive
Because it suggests foraging is the most important and experienced role, earned after completing simpler tasks
Because forager bees go to a special school
Because only the queen bee decides who becomes a forager
questions.correctAnswer: Because it suggests foraging is the most important and experienced role, earned after completing simpler tasks
questions.explanation: The word 'graduate' is usually associated with completing a course of study and earning a higher position. By using this word, the author implies that the forager role is the highest-level job in the hive, one that requires experience. The passage shows that a bee must first work as a cleaner, nurse and builder before it is old enough and experienced enough to become a forager. ✓

questions.question: What does the word 'vigorously' mean in the phrase 'fan it vigorously with their wings'?
options: Gently and carefully
Slowly and lazily
With great energy and force
In a circular motion
One at a time
questions.correctAnswer: With great energy and force
questions.explanation: In this context, 'vigorously' means with great energy and force. The bees need to fan the nectar hard enough to evaporate the water from it, so they must beat their wings with considerable strength and speed. The word emphasises how much effort is involved in this stage of honey-making. ✓

questions.question: Why does the author end the passage by discussing pollination rather than just honey production?
options: Because the author forgot to mention it earlier
To show that honey is not very important
To persuade the reader that bees matter to all of us, not just honey lovers
To explain how to grow flowers in a garden
To compare bees with butterflies
questions.correctAnswer: To persuade the reader that bees matter to all of us, not just honey lovers
questions.explanation: The author deliberately ends with pollination to broaden the reader's understanding of why bees are important. While honey production is interesting, pollination affects everyone because one third of our food depends on it. By ending on this point, the author ensures the reader understands that protecting bees is not just about honey — it is about our entire food supply. ✓

questions.question: The author uses human job titles like 'cleaner', 'nurse' and 'builder' to describe the bees' roles. What effect does this have on the reader?
options: It makes the reader think bees are actually human
It confuses the reader about what bees really do
It helps the reader understand and relate to the bees' roles by comparing them to familiar human jobs
It shows that humans copied their jobs from bees
It proves that bees are more intelligent than humans
questions.correctAnswer: It helps the reader understand and relate to the bees' roles by comparing them to familiar human jobs
questions.explanation: By giving the bees human job titles, the author uses personification to make the bees' behaviour easier to understand. Readers can immediately picture what a 'cleaner' or 'nurse' does because these are familiar roles. This technique also creates a sense of admiration for the bees' organisation, as it suggests their hive operates like a well-run workplace where everyone has an important contribution to make. ✓

questions.question: According to the passage, what did the blacksmith make while working beneath the oak tree?
options: Swords and shields
Horseshoes
Gates and fences
Ploughs
Nails and chains
questions.correctAnswer: Horseshoes
questions.explanation: The passage states that 'a blacksmith worked beneath my branches, the ring of his hammer echoing across the fields as he shaped horseshoes on his anvil.' This is directly stated in the second paragraph. ✓

questions.question: How many adults does it take to encircle the oak tree's trunk with outstretched arms?
options: Three adults
Four adults
Five adults
Six adults
Seven adults
questions.correctAnswer: Five adults
questions.explanation: The passage directly states that 'My trunk thickened until it took five adults standing in a ring with outstretched arms to encircle it.' This information is clearly given in the third paragraph. ✓

questions.question: What can we infer about how the village has changed over the four hundred years?
options: It has stayed exactly the same since the oak was planted
It has grown from a tiny settlement into a larger, modern community
It has become smaller and less populated
It has been destroyed and rebuilt many times
It has moved to a different location
questions.correctAnswer: It has grown from a tiny settlement into a larger, modern community
questions.explanation: The passage does not directly say the village has grown, but we can infer this from several clues. It began as 'a handful of thatched cottages clustered around a muddy track,' but by the present day it has a village green, a council that places preservation orders, and schoolchildren who visit on nature walks. The mention of 'this modern age' also suggests significant development over the centuries. ✓

questions.question: What does the word 'weathered' mean in the phrase 'I have weathered every storm'?
options: Enjoyed
Predicted
Created
Survived and endured
Avoided completely
questions.correctAnswer: Survived and endured
questions.explanation: In this context, 'weathered' means survived and endured. The oak tree is saying it has lived through every storm, even though some caused damage. The phrase 'though not without scars' confirms that 'weathered' means getting through something difficult rather than avoiding it altogether. ✓

questions.question: Why did the author choose to tell this story from the tree's point of view rather than from a human narrator?
options: Because trees can actually talk and share their memories
To show that the tree is angry about being damaged by storms
To give the reader a unique perspective on the passage of time and create an emotional connection with nature
Because it was easier to write than using a human character
To teach the reader scientific facts about how trees grow
questions.correctAnswer: To give the reader a unique perspective on the passage of time and create an emotional connection with nature
questions.explanation: By telling the story from the tree's perspective, the author allows the reader to experience four hundred years of history through a single, continuous voice. This first-person narration creates an emotional bond between the reader and the tree, making us care about its survival. It also highlights how nature witnesses and outlasts human generations, giving the passage a reflective, almost magical quality. ✓

questions.question: The author describes the tree as 'a living library, holding the memory of this place in every ring of my ancient trunk.' What literary device is being used and what does it suggest?
options: Alliteration — it makes the sentence sound musical
A simile — it compares the tree to a building
A metaphor — it suggests the tree holds knowledge and history, just as a library holds books
Onomatopoeia — it imitates the sound of turning pages
Repetition — it repeats the word 'library' for emphasis
questions.correctAnswer: A metaphor — it suggests the tree holds knowledge and history, just as a library holds books
questions.explanation: The author uses a metaphor, comparing the tree directly to a 'living library.' Just as a library stores books full of information and stories, the tree's growth rings store centuries of history. Each ring represents one year of the tree's life — one more chapter in its story. This metaphor suggests the tree is valuable not just as a living thing, but as a keeper of the village's entire past. ✓

questions.question: In what year did the Great Storm hit southern England?
questions.explanation: The passage clearly states that 'On the morning of 16th October 1987' Michael Fish made his famous forecast, and the storm struck that same night. The year 1987 is directly stated in the opening paragraph. ✓

questions.question: Where is the Met Office based?
options: London
Manchester
Exeter
Edinburgh
Birmingham
questions.correctAnswer: Exeter
questions.explanation: The passage directly states that 'The Met Office, which is based in Exeter, operates one of the most powerful supercomputers in the world.' This is clearly stated in the third paragraph. ✓

questions.question: Why do you think the 1987 storm 'changed the way Britain thinks about weather forecasting forever'?
options: Because people stopped watching weather forecasts after that
Because it proved that Britain never gets bad weather
Because the failure to predict such a destructive storm showed that forecasting needed to be dramatically improved
Because Michael Fish was promoted after his correct prediction
Because the storm was not actually very serious
questions.correctAnswer: Because the failure to predict such a destructive storm showed that forecasting needed to be dramatically improved
questions.explanation: The passage describes how Michael Fish reassured viewers there was no hurricane coming, yet hours later the worst storm in 300 years struck. This massive failure to predict a devastating event — fifteen million trees uprooted, buildings flattened, millions without power — would have shocked the public and motivated the government and meteorological services to invest heavily in better forecasting technology. The reader can infer that this embarrassing mistake drove major improvements. ✓

questions.question: What does the word 'arsenal' mean in the phrase 'a modern meteorologist's arsenal'?
options: A football stadium
A collection of weapons or tools available for use
A type of weather station
A university department
A storage cupboard
questions.correctAnswer: A collection of weapons or tools available for use
questions.explanation: In this context, 'arsenal' means a collection of tools or resources available for a particular purpose. Although 'arsenal' originally referred to a store of weapons, the author uses it here to describe the range of technological tools that meteorologists have at their disposal — satellites, radar and supercomputers. It suggests that forecasters are well-equipped to fight against the challenge of predicting weather. ✓

questions.question: Why does the author begin the passage with the story of Michael Fish and the 1987 storm?
options: To make fun of Michael Fish for getting the forecast wrong
To show that weather forecasting has always been perfect
To grab the reader's attention with a dramatic, real-life event and set up the theme of how forecasting has improved
To explain why people should not watch the news
To argue that storms are not dangerous
questions.correctAnswer: To grab the reader's attention with a dramatic, real-life event and set up the theme of how forecasting has improved
questions.explanation: The author opens with the dramatic story of Michael Fish's incorrect forecast and the devastating storm that followed because it immediately grabs the reader's attention and creates a compelling hook. It also establishes the central theme of the passage: that weather forecasting has improved enormously since that failure. By starting with this famous mistake, the author creates a clear 'before and after' structure that makes the progress described later feel more impressive. ✓

questions.question: The author writes that warm air 'collides' with cold air. How does this word choice help the reader understand British weather?
options: It suggests the air masses gently blend together
It creates a violent, forceful image that helps explain why the weather can change so suddenly and dramatically
It implies that air masses move very slowly
It shows that British weather is always calm
It compares the weather to a car crash
questions.correctAnswer: It creates a violent, forceful image that helps explain why the weather can change so suddenly and dramatically
questions.explanation: The word 'collides' is a powerful, forceful verb that creates an image of two opposing forces crashing into each other. This word choice helps the reader understand that when warm Atlantic air meets cold Arctic air, the result is not gentle or gradual — it is sudden and dramatic, which explains why British weather can change so quickly and unpredictably. The violent imagery mirrors the disruptive effect these collisions have on our weather. ✓

questions.question: What was the Roman name for the settlement at Bath?
options: Aquae Roma
Aquae Sulis
Aquae Britannia
Aquae Minerva
Aquae Therma
questions.correctAnswer: Aquae Sulis
questions.explanation: The passage directly states that the Romans 'named the settlement Aquae Sulis, meaning the waters of Sulis.' This is a retrieval question where the answer is clearly given in the text. ✓
questions.passageTitle: The Roman Baths of Aquae Sulis

questions.question: What was the temperature of the water that rises from underground?
options: About thirty-six degrees Celsius
About forty degrees Celsius
About forty-six degrees Celsius
About fifty degrees Celsius
About twenty-six degrees Celsius
questions.correctAnswer: About forty-six degrees Celsius
questions.explanation: The passage states the water 'rises from deep underground at a temperature of around forty-six degrees Celsius.' This information is directly stated in the text. ✓
questions.passageTitle: The Roman Baths of Aquae Sulis

questions.question: Why would the hot springs have been especially appealing to the Romans in Britain?
options: Because the Romans had never seen hot water before
Because the Romans came from a warmer climate and found British winters difficult
Because the Romans needed to wash their togas
Because the springs were free to use
Because the Romans were afraid of cold water
questions.correctAnswer: Because the Romans came from a warmer climate and found British winters difficult
questions.explanation: The passage says the warm water 'would have felt wonderfully warm to the Romans, especially during the cold British winters they were not accustomed to.' This tells us they came from a warmer place (Italy) and were not used to the cold British climate. ✓
questions.passageTitle: The Roman Baths of Aquae Sulis

questions.question: What does the word 'invigorate' mean in the phrase 'designed to close the pores and invigorate the body'?
options: To make tired and sleepy
To make cold and numb
To refresh and fill with energy
To clean and polish
To stretch and relax
questions.correctAnswer: To refresh and fill with energy
questions.explanation: In this context, 'invigorate' means to refresh and fill with energy. The cold plunge pool was meant to wake the body up and make bathers feel lively after the heat of the hot rooms. ✓
questions.passageTitle: The Roman Baths of Aquae Sulis

questions.question: Why does the author describe merchants, politicians, and children at the baths?
options: To show that the baths were only for wealthy people
To prove that Romans were lazy and spent too much time bathing
To show that the baths were a place for the whole community, not just for washing
To explain that children were not allowed in the hot pools
To argue that Roman baths were better than modern swimming pools
questions.correctAnswer: To show that the baths were a place for the whole community, not just for washing
questions.explanation: By mentioning merchants discussing business, politicians debating, and children playing, the author shows that the baths served as a social hub for the entire community. This supports the earlier point that 'bathing was not simply about getting clean.' ✓
questions.passageTitle: The Roman Baths of Aquae Sulis

questions.question: How does the author use the structure of the passage to guide the reader through the topic?
options: By telling the story in reverse, starting with the present day
By moving chronologically from the Roman arrival, through daily life, to the modern rediscovery
By comparing Roman baths to modern swimming pools throughout
By listing facts in alphabetical order
By repeating the same information in different ways
questions.correctAnswer: By moving chronologically from the Roman arrival, through daily life, to the modern rediscovery
questions.explanation: The author structures the passage chronologically: first the Romans discovering the springs, then describing daily bathing life, then the temple, then the Romans leaving in 410 AD, and finally the eighteenth-century rediscovery. This timeline structure helps the reader follow the full history clearly. ✓
questions.passageTitle: The Roman Baths of Aquae Sulis

questions.question: What material was the replica longship built from?
options: Pine planks
Overlapping planks of oak
Birch logs
Elm beams
Ash boards
questions.correctAnswer: Overlapping planks of oak
questions.explanation: The passage states the ship was built using 'overlapping planks of oak, held together with iron rivets.' This is directly stated in the information panel that Amir's grandmother reads. ✓

questions.question: What was fixed to the right-hand side of the ship at the stern?
options: A carved dragon's head
A large anchor
A single large steering oar
A wooden shield
A lantern
questions.correctAnswer: A single large steering oar
questions.explanation: The passage states 'a single large steering oar was fixed to the right-hand side of the ship' at the stern. This is directly stated in the text. ✓

questions.question: What does the final paragraph suggest about the effect the ship has on Amir?
options: He is bored and wants to go home
He is frightened by the dragon's head
He is deeply inspired and wants to remember every detail
He is upset because he cannot touch the ship
He is confused about how the ship was built
questions.correctAnswer: He is deeply inspired and wants to remember every detail
questions.explanation: Amir wants to 'memorise every plank, every rivet, every curve' and the narrator says the ship 'could still take your breath away.' His shining eyes and desire to stay longer all show he is deeply inspired and moved by the experience. ✓

questions.question: What does the word 'billowing' mean in the phrase 'the striped woollen sail billowing above him'?
options: Torn and ragged
Folded neatly
Swelling and puffing out with wind
Hanging limply
Spinning in circles
questions.correctAnswer: Swelling and puffing out with wind
questions.explanation: In this context, 'billowing' means swelling outwards and puffing up as it fills with wind. It creates a vivid image of the sail catching the breeze and curving outward as the ship moves through the sea. ✓

questions.question: Why does the author blend Amir's imagination with factual information about Vikings?
options: To confuse the reader about what is real and what is imagined
To show that Amir is not paying attention to the facts
To bring history to life by showing how it can capture a child's imagination
To prove that museums are boring without imagination
To suggest that Amir wants to become a sailor
questions.correctAnswer: To bring history to life by showing how it can capture a child's imagination
questions.explanation: By weaving Amir's vivid imaginings (salt spray, chanting crew) with real historical facts (navigation by stars, sunstones, the origin of 'starboard'), the author shows how history can spark a child's imagination and make the past feel exciting and alive. ✓

questions.question: The author describes the ship's hull as 'curving upwards like the wings of a giant bird.' What technique is this and what effect does it create?
options: Alliteration — it makes the words sound rhythmic
A simile — it helps the reader picture the graceful, sweeping shape of the ship
Personification — it makes the ship seem alive
Repetition — it emphasises how big the ship is
Onomatopoeia — it imitates the sound of the ship
questions.correctAnswer: A simile — it helps the reader picture the graceful, sweeping shape of the ship
questions.explanation: This is a simile because it compares the hull to bird's wings using the word 'like.' The comparison helps the reader visualise the elegant, sweeping curve of the longship and gives it a sense of beauty and grace. ✓

questions.question: How many volunteer nurses did Florence Nightingale take to Scutari?
options: Twenty-eight
Thirty-two
Thirty-eight
Forty-eight
Fifty-eight
questions.correctAnswer: Thirty-eight
questions.explanation: The passage states that Florence 'gathered a team of thirty-eight volunteer nurses and sailed to the British military hospital at Scutari.' This is directly stated in the text. ✓

questions.question: Where did Florence Nightingale establish the first professional training school for nurses?
options: Scutari Hospital
The Crimea
St Thomas' Hospital in London
A hospital in Florence
Oxford University
questions.correctAnswer: St Thomas' Hospital in London
questions.explanation: The passage states she 'founded the first professional training school for nurses at St Thomas' Hospital in London.' This information is directly given in the final paragraph. ✓

questions.question: Why did many army doctors resist Nightingale's changes at first?
options: They thought her ideas about hygiene were wrong
They resented a woman challenging their authority and methods
They were too busy fighting the war
They did not have enough money for clean supplies
They thought the soldiers preferred dirty conditions
questions.correctAnswer: They resented a woman challenging their authority and methods
questions.explanation: The passage says doctors viewed her as 'an interfering outsider.' Combined with the earlier mention that many believed nursing was 'not a suitable profession for a woman of her background,' we can infer the doctors resented a woman coming in and telling them how to do their jobs. ✓

questions.question: What does the word 'contaminated' mean in the phrase 'the water supply was contaminated'?
options: Running low and nearly empty
Perfectly clean and safe to drink
Made impure or unsafe by harmful substances
Frozen solid because of cold weather
Shared between too many people
questions.correctAnswer: Made impure or unsafe by harmful substances
questions.explanation: In this context, 'contaminated' means polluted or made impure by harmful substances. The hospital was built over a blocked sewer, so the water was likely full of bacteria and waste, making it unsafe to drink. ✓

questions.question: Why does the author begin the passage by describing the terrible conditions at the hospital before introducing Florence Nightingale?
options: To make the reader feel sorry for the author
To show that the war was not important
To establish how serious the problem was, making Nightingale's achievements more impressive
To persuade the reader that wars should be stopped
To fill space before getting to the main story
questions.correctAnswer: To establish how serious the problem was, making Nightingale's achievements more impressive
questions.explanation: By describing the dreadful conditions first — filthy hospitals, soldiers dying from disease, rats between beds — the author makes the reader understand the scale of the problem. This makes Nightingale's determination and her dramatic improvements all the more impressive and admirable. ✓

questions.question: What is the effect of the image of Florence's lamp moving through the dark wards at night?
options: It suggests the hospital had no electricity, which is just a practical detail
It symbolises hope and care in the midst of suffering and darkness
It shows that Florence was afraid of the dark
It proves that the hospital was very large
It explains how oil lamps work
questions.correctAnswer: It symbolises hope and care in the midst of suffering and darkness
questions.explanation: The image of the lamp moving through the darkness is symbolic. The 'darkness' represents the suffering, fear, and death in the hospital, while the 'light' represents hope, comfort, and care. The soldiers said 'simply seeing her light gave them hope,' showing the lamp symbolises something far deeper than just a source of light. ✓

questions.question: How long was the Silk Road network?
options: More than two thousand miles
More than four thousand miles
More than six thousand miles
More than eight thousand miles
More than ten thousand miles
questions.correctAnswer: More than six thousand miles
questions.explanation: The passage states the Silk Road was 'a network of dusty tracks stretching more than six thousand miles.' This information is directly given in the opening sentence. ✓

questions.question: What punishment did the Chinese impose on anyone caught smuggling silkworms out of the country?
options: A large fine
Imprisonment for life
Being sent into exile
Severe punishment
Loss of their property
questions.correctAnswer: Severe punishment
questions.explanation: The passage states that 'anyone caught smuggling silkworms or their eggs out of the country faced severe punishment.' This is directly stated in the text. ✓

questions.question: Why did few merchants ever complete the entire Silk Road journey?
options: Because they were not allowed to cross national borders
Because the journey was far too long and dangerous for one person to manage alone
Because they preferred to stay in the market towns
Because the Chinese government stopped them
Because the road was only open for part of the year
questions.correctAnswer: Because the journey was far too long and dangerous for one person to manage alone
questions.explanation: The passage describes scorching deserts, towering mountains, thin air, snowbound passes, and bandits. These extreme dangers, combined with the vast distance of six thousand miles, made it practically impossible for any single merchant to complete the whole route safely. Instead, goods were 'passed from trader to trader.' ✓

questions.question: What does the word 'legacy' mean in the phrase 'the most lasting legacy of the Silk Road'?
options: A type of cargo carried on camels
A dangerous section of a trade route
Something valuable handed down from the past
A written record of events
A meeting place for merchants
questions.correctAnswer: Something valuable handed down from the past
questions.explanation: In this context, 'legacy' means something valuable that has been passed down or left behind by those who came before. The author is saying that the most important thing the Silk Road left to future generations was not goods but the exchange of knowledge. ✓

questions.question: Why does the author save the exchange of knowledge until the final paragraph?
options: Because the author forgot to mention it earlier
Because knowledge is less interesting than silk and spices
To build towards the most important point, ending with the idea that knowledge mattered more than any physical goods
To fill up space at the end of the passage
Because the exchange of knowledge happened last in history
questions.correctAnswer: To build towards the most important point, ending with the idea that knowledge mattered more than any physical goods
questions.explanation: The author deliberately structures the passage to build towards the final, most important point. After describing all the valuable physical goods traded, the author reveals that 'the most lasting legacy' was actually the exchange of knowledge. Placing this at the end gives it extra emphasis and leaves the reader with the passage's key message. ✓

questions.question: The author opens with 'Imagine a network of dusty tracks stretching more than six thousand miles.' What technique is this and why is it effective?
options: A rhetorical question — it makes the reader think of an answer
Alliteration — it uses repeated sounds to create rhythm
Direct address — it invites the reader to picture the scene, making the history feel vivid and personal
A metaphor — it compares the Silk Road to something else
Onomatopoeia — it imitates the sound of travelling
questions.correctAnswer: Direct address — it invites the reader to picture the scene, making the history feel vivid and personal
questions.explanation: By using the imperative 'Imagine,' the author speaks directly to the reader and invites them to create a mental picture. This technique of direct address draws the reader in immediately, making the ancient trade route feel vivid, real, and personal rather than distant and dry. ✓

questions.question: What was on Sir Edward Ashworth's coat of arms?
options: A golden lion on a red shield
A silver stag on a blue shield
A white horse on a green shield
A black eagle on a yellow shield
A red dragon on a white shield
questions.correctAnswer: A silver stag on a blue shield
questions.explanation: The passage states that the tunic was 'embroidered with Sir Edward's coat of arms — a silver stag on a blue shield.' This is directly stated in the text. ✓

questions.question: What time was dinner eaten in the Tudor household?
options: Nine o'clock in the morning
Eleven o'clock in the morning
One o'clock in the afternoon
Three o'clock in the afternoon
Six o'clock in the evening
questions.correctAnswer: Eleven o'clock in the morning
questions.explanation: The passage states that 'dinner, which in Tudor times was eaten at eleven o'clock in the morning.' This is directly stated in the text. ✓

questions.question: What can we infer about Thomas's feelings towards his role as a page?
options: He hates every moment and wants to go home
He finds it physically demanding but feels proud and accepts the challenges
He thinks it is too easy and wishes for harder work
He is planning to run away as soon as possible
He is only there because his father forced him and he has no interest
questions.correctAnswer: He finds it physically demanding but feels proud and accepts the challenges
questions.explanation: Thomas is proud of his tunic, he 'did not dare complain' about aching arms, and he agrees with Robert that it is 'not a bad life' — despite being sore, tired, and ink-stained. This shows he accepts the hardships because he values the opportunity and feels pride in his position. ✓

questions.question: What does the word 'advance' mean in the phrase 'a page who grumbled was a page who would never advance'?
options: Move forward physically across the courtyard
Be promoted to a higher position
Receive extra food at dinner
Be allowed to go home early
Learn to read and write
questions.correctAnswer: Be promoted to a higher position
questions.explanation: In this context, 'advance' means to be promoted or to move up in rank. Thomas's father hopes he will 'eventually become a squire,' so 'advance' refers to progressing from page to squire — the next step in a young gentleman's training. ✓

questions.question: Why does the author include details like the time of dinner, the items on the table, and Thomas's breakfast?
options: To make the reader feel hungry
To show that Tudor people ate too much food
To bring the Tudor period to life by showing how different daily routines were from today
To prove that Thomas was a fussy eater
To compare Tudor food with modern school dinners
questions.correctAnswer: To bring the Tudor period to life by showing how different daily routines were from today
questions.explanation: By including specific historical details — eating dinner at eleven, drinking small beer, using pewter plates and silver salt cellars — the author helps the reader understand how different everyday life was in Tudor times. These authentic details make the historical setting feel real and vivid. ✓

questions.question: What effect does the final line — 'Not a bad life at all' — have on the reader?
options: It makes the reader feel sorry for Thomas because he is clearly lying
It creates a warm, satisfying ending by showing Thomas has found contentment despite the hardships
It suggests Thomas is going to complain to Sir Edward
It shows that Thomas does not understand how hard his life is
It tells the reader that all pages enjoyed their work
questions.correctAnswer: It creates a warm, satisfying ending by showing Thomas has found contentment despite the hardships
questions.explanation: The final line echoes Robert's words but with Thomas's own quiet agreement. After listing all his physical discomforts — sore feet, ink-stained fingers, aching arms — the understated 'Not a bad life at all' creates a warm ending that shows Thomas has found genuine contentment and belonging in his new role. The understatement makes his acceptance feel more real and touching. ✓

questions.question: What was the name of Elara's cat?
options: Midnight
Cinnamon
Biscuit
Amber
Whiskers
questions.correctAnswer: Biscuit
questions.explanation: The passage states 'Elara's cat, Biscuit, slipped through a gap in the fence.' The cat's name is Biscuit. ✓

questions.question: How many different books did Elara explore inside the library?
options: One
Two
Three
Four
Five
questions.correctAnswer: Three
questions.explanation: The passage says 'She spent what felt like hours exploring three different books, each one a doorway into another world.' She explored three books. ✓

questions.question: What does the message 'WE HAVE BEEN WAITING' suggest about the library?
options: The library had been expecting a visitor for a long time
The library was annoyed that Elara was late
The books were bored and wanted entertainment
Someone had left a note for Elara earlier that day
The candles had been burning since the morning
questions.correctAnswer: The library had been expecting a visitor for a long time
questions.explanation: The golden words say 'WE HAVE BEEN WAITING,' which implies the library was somehow aware of Elara and had been expecting her to come for a long time. This suggests the library has a mysterious, knowing quality. ✓

questions.question: In the phrase 'a thick curtain of ivy smothered the walls,' what does the word 'smothered' most closely mean?
options: Decorated
Covered completely
Damaged
Touched lightly
Protected
questions.correctAnswer: Covered completely
questions.explanation: 'Smothered' means to cover something thickly or completely. Here, the ivy has grown so densely over the walls that it covers them entirely, like a blanket. ✓

questions.question: Why does the author describe the building as a 'crumbling ruin' at the start of the passage?
options: To show that the building is dangerous and should be demolished
To create a contrast between its dull outside appearance and the magical world within
To explain why no one has repaired the building for years
To make the reader feel sorry for Elara's village
To suggest that the library has only recently appeared
questions.correctAnswer: To create a contrast between its dull outside appearance and the magical world within
questions.explanation: The author describes the building as a ruin so that when Elara steps inside and finds a glittering, magical library, the contrast is striking and surprising. This makes the discovery feel even more wonderful. ✓

questions.question: Which literary device is used in the phrase 'It fluttered open like a bird spreading its wings'?
options: Alliteration
Metaphor
Simile
Onomatopoeia
Personification
questions.correctAnswer: Simile
questions.explanation: This is a simile because it uses the word 'like' to compare the book opening to a bird spreading its wings. A simile compares two different things using 'like' or 'as.' ✓

questions.question: Where were Mia and Sam staying for the summer holidays?
options: With their uncle in Cornwall
With their grandparents in a Dorset village
At a holiday camp near the seaside
With their cousins in London
At a boarding school in the countryside
questions.correctAnswer: With their grandparents in a Dorset village
questions.explanation: The passage states that 'Mia and her younger brother Sam had been sent to stay with their grandparents in the small Dorset village of Brindleton.' ✓

questions.question: What was hidden behind the loose brick in the wall?
options: A rolled-up map
A tin box
A small brass key
A hand-painted postcard
A compass
questions.correctAnswer: A small brass key
questions.explanation: The passage says 'a crumbling stone wall behind the baker's shop, where a loose brick hid a small brass key.' The hidden object was a small brass key. ✓

questions.question: What can you infer about Great-Aunt Hattie from the contents of the tin box?
options: She was a nervous person who rarely left home
She was a well-travelled person who loved adventure
She worked as a postcard artist in the village
She was a teacher who wanted children to study geography
She was a baker who lived behind the shop
questions.correctAnswer: She was a well-travelled person who loved adventure
questions.explanation: The tin box contained postcards from places around the world, a compass, and a note signed 'Your loving adventurer.' These clues together suggest that Great-Aunt Hattie was someone who travelled widely and loved adventure. ✓

questions.question: In the sentence 'The attic was a treasure trove of forgotten things,' what does the phrase 'treasure trove' mean?
options: A locked safe full of gold coins
A collection of valuable or interesting items
A hidden underground cave
A shop selling antiques
A pirate's buried chest
questions.correctAnswer: A collection of valuable or interesting items
questions.explanation: 'Treasure trove' is used here to mean a collection of valuable or interesting items. The attic is full of fascinating old objects, so the author uses this phrase to show it is a rich store of discoveries. ✓

questions.question: Why does the author end the passage with the sentence 'The summer holidays had only just begun'?
options: To remind the reader what time of year it is
To suggest that Mia and Sam will have many more adventures ahead
To show that the children were bored and had nothing else to do
To explain that the treasure hunt took only one morning
To warn the reader that the story is about to become sad
questions.correctAnswer: To suggest that Mia and Sam will have many more adventures ahead
questions.explanation: By ending with 'The summer holidays had only just begun,' the author hints that this treasure hunt is just the start and that many more exciting adventures lie ahead for Mia and Sam. It leaves the reader feeling hopeful and curious. ✓

questions.question: The stone is called 'the Whispering Stone' and Mia hears 'a faint hum — almost like whispering.' What literary device is used to describe the stone?
options: Simile
Alliteration
Personification
Repetition
Hyperbole
questions.correctAnswer: Personification
questions.explanation: Personification is giving human qualities to a non-human thing. Stones cannot whisper, but the author describes the stone as if it can, giving it the human ability to make whispering sounds. ✓

questions.question: What is the name of Cadi's border collie?
options: Draig
Ffion
Fflur
Blodwen
Seren
questions.correctAnswer: Fflur
questions.explanation: The passage states that Cadi climbed the slopes 'with her border collie, Fflur, trotting at her heels.' The dog's name is Fflur. ✓

questions.question: What did Cadi bring to Draig on her Saturday visits?
options: Food from her kitchen
Smooth river stones
Books from the village library
Drawings she had made
Firewood to keep him warm
questions.correctAnswer: Books from the village library
questions.explanation: The passage says 'She brought him books from the village library, and in return he told her stories of ancient Wales.' Cadi brought books. ✓

questions.question: Why does Draig say 'That has not happened in over three hundred years' when Cadi sees him?
options: He has not eaten in three hundred years
He has not been able to fly for three hundred years
No human has been able to see him for over three hundred years
He has not spoken to another dragon in three hundred years
The cave has not had a visitor in three hundred years
questions.correctAnswer: No human has been able to see him for over three hundred years
questions.explanation: Draig says 'You can see me' and then 'That has not happened in over three hundred years.' This implies that other people have been nearby but could not see him — only Cadi has the ability to perceive him, and this has not happened for centuries. ✓

questions.question: In the sentence 'One amber eye opened and regarded her calmly,' what does the word 'regarded' mean?
options: Ignored
Frightened
Looked at
Blinked at
Challenged
questions.correctAnswer: Looked at
questions.explanation: 'Regarded' means looked at or observed. The dragon opened one eye and looked at Cadi calmly, studying her without alarm or aggression. ✓

questions.question: Why does the author include the detail that Draig tells Cadi 'stories of ancient Wales — of princes, battles, and a time when dragons filled the skies'?
options: To show that Draig is a boastful creature who likes to exaggerate
To prove that the story is set in the past
To highlight that Draig is a living link to Welsh history and legend
To explain why Cadi is falling behind at school
To suggest that Draig is making up stories to entertain himself
questions.correctAnswer: To highlight that Draig is a living link to Welsh history and legend
questions.explanation: The author includes these details to show that Draig is not just a dragon but a keeper of Welsh history and mythology. He is a living connection to a legendary past, which makes his survival — and his friendship with Cadi — all the more meaningful. ✓

questions.question: What literary device is used in the phrase 'dragons filled the skies like starlings at dusk'?
options: Personification
Metaphor
Onomatopoeia
Simile
Alliteration
questions.correctAnswer: Simile
questions.explanation: This is a simile because it uses the word 'like' to compare the dragons filling the sky to starlings (a common bird) gathering at dusk. The comparison helps the reader picture a sky crowded with dragons. ✓

questions.question: What was the name of the yacht they were sailing on?
options: The Storm Chaser
The Cornish Maid
The Silver Gull
The White Falcon
The Sea Breeze
questions.correctAnswer: The Silver Gull
questions.explanation: The passage says 'The yacht, a sleek white vessel called the Silver Gull, cut through the waves off the Cornish coast.' The yacht was called the Silver Gull. ✓

questions.question: What did Priya and Marcus discover while exploring the island?
options: A ruined lighthouse and a cave
A freshwater stream and a sheltered hollow
A fishing boat and a village
A radio tower and a path
A sandy cove and a rock pool
questions.correctAnswer: A freshwater stream and a sheltered hollow
questions.explanation: The passage states 'Priya and Marcus explored the island, discovering a freshwater stream trickling down from the rocks and a sheltered hollow out of the wind.' ✓

questions.question: Why did Finn throw damp seaweed onto the fire?
options: To put the fire out before leaving the island
To cook food over the flames
To create visible smoke that would signal the rescue boat
To keep insects away from the group
To make the fire burn hotter and brighter
questions.correctAnswer: To create visible smoke that would signal the rescue boat
questions.explanation: Finn threw damp seaweed on the fire when Zara spotted the coastguard vessel. Damp seaweed creates thick white smoke, which acts as a signal to the rescue boat so they could be seen and found. ✓

questions.question: In the sentence 'Captain Trevelyan barked orders,' what does the word 'barked' suggest about how he spoke?
options: He spoke quietly so no one could hear
He spoke in a gentle, encouraging way
He shouted sharply and urgently
He was imitating the sound of a dog
He was singing the instructions
questions.correctAnswer: He shouted sharply and urgently
questions.explanation: 'Barked' is used here to suggest that the captain shouted his orders sharply and urgently, like a short, loud bark. It conveys the panic and danger of the storm situation. ✓

questions.question: Why does the author choose to give 'Then the storm arrived' its own short paragraph?
options: Because there was nothing else to describe about the weather
To create a dramatic pause that signals a sudden change in the story
To show that the storm lasted a very short time
Because the author ran out of things to say about the calm sailing
To separate the introduction from the conclusion
questions.correctAnswer: To create a dramatic pause that signals a sudden change in the story
questions.explanation: Placing 'Then the storm arrived' in its own short paragraph creates a dramatic pause. It signals to the reader that the mood is about to shift suddenly from calm enjoyment to danger, building tension and suspense. ✓

questions.question: Which literary device is used in 'a wall of charcoal cloud that swallowed the horizon'?
options: Simile
Onomatopoeia
Metaphor
Alliteration
Rhyme
questions.correctAnswer: Metaphor
questions.explanation: This is a metaphor because the cloud is described as being a 'wall' and as 'swallowing' the horizon, without using 'like' or 'as.' The cloud is not literally a wall and cannot literally swallow anything, but these comparisons vividly convey the storm's power and speed. ✓

questions.question: Where was Mr Thorne's shop located?
options: On the high street next to the library
Between the chemist and the post office
Opposite the town hall
At the end of Birch Lane
Inside the shopping centre
questions.correctAnswer: Between the chemist and the post office
questions.explanation: The passage says 'the narrow shop squeezed between the chemist and the post office.' Mr Thorne's shop was located between these two businesses. ✓

questions.question: How long did Nell spend straightening the bent tooth on a brass cog?
options: Thirty minutes
One hour
Two hours
Three hours
Five hours
questions.correctAnswer: Three hours
questions.explanation: The passage says 'One afternoon, Nell spent three hours straightening a single bent tooth on a brass cog.' It took her three hours. ✓

questions.question: What can you infer from the detail that Nell said 'I wasn't going to' touch anything, 'though her fingers were already itching'?
options: Nell was allergic to the dust in the shop
Nell was nervous and did not want to be in the shop
Nell was naturally curious and eager to handle the clocks
Nell was trying to be polite but found the clocks boring
Nell had a skin condition on her hands
questions.correctAnswer: Nell was naturally curious and eager to handle the clocks
questions.explanation: Although Nell claimed she would not touch anything, the fact that 'her fingers were already itching' tells us she was desperate to pick things up and examine them. This shows she is naturally curious and drawn to the craft, even on her very first visit. ✓

questions.question: In the description of Mr Thorne, what does the word 'nimble' mean in 'fingers as nimble as a pianist's'?
options: Large and strong
Quick and skilful
Rough and calloused
Thin and bony
Stiff and awkward
questions.correctAnswer: Quick and skilful
questions.explanation: 'Nimble' means quick, light and skilful in movement. Mr Thorne's fingers are compared to a pianist's because both need to move with speed and precision when doing delicate work. ✓

questions.question: Why does the author include Mr Thorne's speech about clockmaking not being 'about telling the time'?
options: To show that Mr Thorne does not know how modern technology works
To reveal the deeper message of the story — that patience, care and craftsmanship matter
To explain why Mr Thorne's shop is not very popular
To persuade Nell to buy one of his clocks
To show that Mr Thorne is an old-fashioned man who dislikes phones
questions.correctAnswer: To reveal the deeper message of the story — that patience, care and craftsmanship matter
questions.explanation: Mr Thorne's speech about 'respecting time' and 'nothing worth making can be rushed' is the heart of the story's message. The author includes it to convey the deeper theme: that patience, care and craftsmanship have lasting value, which is what Nell is learning through her apprenticeship. ✓

questions.question: What literary device is used in 'Grandfather clocks stood like tall soldiers along the back wall'?
options: Metaphor
Personification
Onomatopoeia
Simile
Hyperbole
questions.correctAnswer: Simile
questions.explanation: This is a simile because it uses the word 'like' to compare the grandfather clocks standing in a row to tall soldiers. The comparison helps the reader picture the clocks lined up in an orderly, upright way. ✓

questions.question: What had been growing in the corner of the playing field before the allotment was created?
options: Wildflowers and daisies
Brambles and nettles
Long grass and weeds
Old vegetable plants
Bushes and hedgerows
questions.correctAnswer: Brambles and nettles
questions.explanation: The passage states that parent volunteers 'cleared the overgrown patch of brambles and nettles' during the summer holidays. This is directly stated in the first paragraph. ✓

questions.question: What did Year Six choose to grow in their raised bed?
options: Carrots, radishes, and spring onions
Courgettes, tomatoes, and lettuce
Potatoes and broad beans
Pumpkins, sweetcorn, and runner beans
Strawberries, peas, and herbs
questions.correctAnswer: Pumpkins, sweetcorn, and runner beans
questions.explanation: The passage states that 'Year Six opted for pumpkins, sweetcorn, and runner beans.' This information is found in the third paragraph. ✓

questions.question: Why do you think 'even the children who had grumbled about getting mud under their fingernails' changed their minds about the project?
options: They were forced to take part by their teachers
They enjoyed eating food they had grown themselves and felt proud of their work
They preferred being outdoors to doing lessons
They wanted to impress their parents
They had stopped getting muddy by the end of the project
questions.correctAnswer: They enjoyed eating food they had grown themselves and felt proud of their work
questions.explanation: The passage says these children 'admitted that eating a salad you had grown yourself tasted better than anything from a supermarket.' This suggests they changed their minds because they experienced the pride and satisfaction of eating something they had helped to create. ✓

questions.question: What does the phrase 'threw themselves into the project' mean in the third paragraph?
options: They fell over onto the raised beds
They rushed outside without thinking
They got involved with great energy and eagerness
They argued about what to plant
They competed against each other
questions.correctAnswer: They got involved with great energy and eagerness
questions.explanation: The phrase 'threw themselves into' is an idiom meaning to take part in something with great enthusiasm and energy. The passage confirms this by describing their 'enormous enthusiasm' in the same sentence. ✓

questions.question: Why does the author describe the allotment as having 'become the heart of the school'?
options: Because it was located in the centre of the school grounds
Because it was shaped like a heart
Because it had become the most important and valued part of school life, bringing everyone together
Because the headteacher loved gardening
Because it was the largest area of the school
questions.correctAnswer: Because it had become the most important and valued part of school life, bringing everyone together
questions.explanation: The metaphor 'heart of the school' compares the allotment to a heart — the vital centre that keeps everything alive. The author uses this to show that the garden had become central to school life, bringing together pupils, parents, dinner ladies, and the cook. ✓

questions.question: What evidence is there that the composting process takes a long time?
options: Mrs Kapoor said it would take exactly six months
The bin was only checked once a year
The passage says the mixture would 'slowly break down' into compost
The children forgot to add scraps to the bin
The compost was bought from a garden centre instead
questions.correctAnswer: The passage says the mixture would 'slowly break down' into compost
questions.explanation: The word 'slowly' in the phrase 'the mixture would slowly break down into rich, dark compost' tells us that composting is a gradual process. The author uses this word deliberately to show it takes patience. ✓

questions.question: How many letters per hour can the sorting machine read?
options: Up to 4,000
Up to 10,000
Up to 20,000
Up to 40,000
questions.correctAnswer: Up to 40,000
