questions.explanation: The passage states the parchment was 'tucked inside a battered leather satchel behind a stack of encyclopaedias' in the attic. This is directly stated in the text. ✓

questions.question: Why do you think the colour drained from the grandmother's cheeks when she saw the parchment?
options: She was angry that Jasper had been snooping
She was shocked to see something she thought had been lost long ago
She was frightened of a ghost in the attic
She felt dizzy from climbing the ladder
She was upset because the map was damaged
questions.correctAnswer: She was shocked to see something she thought had been lost long ago
questions.explanation: The grandmother says 'I thought he'd thrown all this away', showing that seeing the parchment was a complete surprise. Her shock comes from unexpectedly encountering a possession belonging to her father or father-in-law that she believed was gone. The physical reaction of colour draining from her face signals deep surprise and emotion. ✓

questions.question: What does the word 'brittle' tell us about the parchment?
options: It was soft and flexible
It was dry and could easily snap or crumble
It was thick and heavy
It was damp from the rain
It was freshly made
questions.correctAnswer: It was dry and could easily snap or crumble
questions.explanation: The word 'brittle' means dry, fragile, and likely to break or crumble. This tells us the parchment is very old and has dried out over time, making it delicate to handle. ✓

questions.question: What do you think Jasper and his grandmother are most likely to do next?
options: Put the map back and forget about it
Sell the map to a museum
Go to Thornley Woods to follow the map's instructions
Phone the police to report the map
Argue about whether the map is real
questions.correctAnswer: Go to Thornley Woods to follow the map's instructions
questions.explanation: The clues all point to an adventure: Jasper asks 'Can I go and look?' with his heart hammering (excitement), and his grandmother says 'I'll get my walking boots' with a youthful smile. They are clearly planning to go to Thornley Woods to follow the map's directions together. ✓

questions.question: The author describes the grandmother's smile as one that 'made her look forty years younger'. What does this literary device suggest?
options: She had actually become younger
The excitement of the adventure made her feel youthful and alive again
She was pretending to be a child
She had put on make-up
She was tired and wanted to rest
questions.correctAnswer: The excitement of the adventure made her feel youthful and alive again
questions.explanation: This is hyperbole (exaggeration for effect). The author does not mean she literally looked forty years younger, but that the excitement and joy of the discovery brought out a youthful energy in her. It suggests the adventure has awakened happy memories and a sense of fun. ✓

questions.question: Why does the author set the story on 'the first day of the summer holidays'?
options: To show that Jasper is lazy and has nothing to do
To explain why the weather is warm
To establish that Jasper has plenty of free time ahead for an adventure
To tell the reader which month it is
To show that school is boring
questions.correctAnswer: To establish that Jasper has plenty of free time ahead for an adventure
questions.explanation: By setting the story on the first day of the summer holidays, the author establishes that Jasper has a long stretch of free time ahead — perfect for following a treasure map. It creates a sense of possibility and suggests the adventure could continue for days or weeks. This is a deliberate authorial choice to make the plot feel believable. ✓

questions.question: At what depth does the wreck of the Silver Gull sit?
options: Ten metres
Twelve metres
Fourteen metres
Sixteen metres
Twenty metres
questions.correctAnswer: Fourteen metres
questions.explanation: The passage states 'The wreck of the Silver Gull lay on the seabed at fourteen metres.' This is directly stated in the text. ✓
questions.passageTitle: The Wreck of the Silver Gull

questions.question: What does the sentence 'But Mia knew she would be back' suggest about her feelings?
options: She was relieved the dive was over
She felt frightened and wanted to overcome her fear
She was deeply fascinated by the wreck and wanted to explore it further
She needed to collect something she had dropped
She was worried the wreck might disappear
questions.correctAnswer: She was deeply fascinated by the wreck and wanted to explore it further
questions.explanation: The final sentence shows Mia's determination to return. Throughout the passage she is captivated by the wreck — swimming closer, looking through the wheelhouse, touching the hull. Her desire to come back shows deep fascination and a sense of wonder, not fear or obligation. ✓
questions.passageTitle: The Wreck of the Silver Gull

questions.question: What does the word 'claimed' mean in the phrase 'the sea had completely claimed it'?
options: Complained about it
Taken ownership of it and made it part of itself
Destroyed it completely
Ignored it
Pushed it to the surface
questions.correctAnswer: Taken ownership of it and made it part of itself
questions.explanation: In this context, 'claimed' means the sea has taken possession of the ship and made it part of the underwater world. The passage supports this — every surface is covered in marine life, showing the sea has absorbed the wreck into its own environment rather than simply destroying it. ✓
questions.passageTitle: The Wreck of the Silver Gull

questions.question: The wreck is described as 'tilted to one side like a sleeping giant'. What technique is the author using and why?
options: Alliteration, to make the sentence sound musical
A simile, to help the reader picture the size and stillness of the wreck
Onomatopoeia, to create a sound effect
Repetition, to emphasise the ship's size
A metaphor, to show the ship is actually alive
questions.correctAnswer: A simile, to help the reader picture the size and stillness of the wreck
questions.explanation: The phrase 'like a sleeping giant' is a simile — a comparison using 'like' or 'as'. It helps the reader picture the wreck as something huge and still, resting quietly on the seabed. The word 'sleeping' also suggests peacefulness rather than destruction, matching the passage's theme of beauty in decay. ✓
questions.passageTitle: The Wreck of the Silver Gull

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT mentioned as something Mia saw on or around the wreck?
options: Barnacles and seaweed
Orange and purple anemones
A large crab on the ship's wheel
A shoal of silver fish
A treasure chest in the cabin
questions.correctAnswer: A treasure chest in the cabin
questions.explanation: The passage mentions barnacles, seaweed, anemones, silver fish, and a crab — but there is no mention of a treasure chest anywhere. This is a negative retrieval question: you must identify the option that is NOT in the text. ✓
questions.passageTitle: The Wreck of the Silver Gull

questions.question: What type of writing is this passage?
options: A newspaper report about a shipwreck
A set of diving instructions
A fictional adventure story
A non-fiction article about marine life
A diary entry from a real person
questions.correctAnswer: A fictional adventure story
questions.explanation: This is a fictional adventure story. It has invented characters (Mia and her father), a narrative structure with a beginning, middle, and end, descriptive language, and a plot centred on exploration. It is not factual reporting, instructions, or a real diary. ✓
questions.passageTitle: The Wreck of the Silver Gull

questions.question: What was painted above the door of the shop?
options: The shop's name in gold letters
A clock face with hands pointing to midnight
A picture of a pocket watch
A sign saying 'Closed'
A painting of an old man
questions.correctAnswer: A clock face with hands pointing to midnight
questions.explanation: The passage states there was 'just a faded painting of a clock face with its hands pointing to midnight' above the door. The shop had no name — only this painted clock. ✓

questions.question: Why do you think the clockmaker looked at Oscar 'with sudden interest' after seeing the watch?
options: He wanted to steal the watch
He recognised it as a rare and special timepiece
He was annoyed that Oscar had disturbed him
He thought the watch was worthless
He was confused by the engraving
questions.correctAnswer: He recognised it as a rare and special timepiece
questions.explanation: The clockmaker immediately identifies the watch as a rare 'Harrington and Sons, 1952' of which only two hundred were made. His sudden interest comes from recognising a valuable and uncommon piece — and possibly knowing a secret about how it works that others do not. ✓

questions.question: What does the word 'mechanism' mean in the phrase 'the mechanism was too old'?
options: The outer case of the watch
The chain attached to the watch
The moving parts inside that make the watch work
The glass covering the watch face
The numbers on the dial
questions.correctAnswer: The moving parts inside that make the watch work
questions.explanation: A 'mechanism' refers to the system of moving parts inside a device that makes it function. In a watch, this means the tiny cogs, springs, and gears inside that drive the hands. The shopping centre watchmaker believed these internal parts were too old to repair. ✓

questions.question: The clockmaker holds the watch to his ear 'as if listening for a heartbeat'. What effect does this comparison create?
options: It suggests the clockmaker is hard of hearing
It makes the watch seem alive, as though it has a life of its own
It shows that the clockmaker is checking if the watch is ticking
It proves the watch is actually a medical instrument
It suggests the clockmaker is pretending to know what he is doing
questions.correctAnswer: It makes the watch seem alive, as though it has a life of its own
questions.explanation: Comparing the watch's ticking to a heartbeat personifies the watch — it gives it human, living qualities. This creates the impression that the watch is not just a machine but something precious and almost alive, which mirrors how Oscar feels about it as a treasured family heirloom. ✓

questions.question: What message is the author conveying through the clockmaker's final words?
options: That modern technology is always better than old things
That knowledge and patience can solve problems that others give up on
That all watches should be taken to specialist shops
That secrets should never be shared with children
That broken things should be thrown away
questions.correctAnswer: That knowledge and patience can solve problems that others give up on
questions.explanation: The clockmaker's words — 'Some things aren't broken. They're just waiting for someone who knows the secret' — carry a deeper meaning. The author is conveying that expertise, patience, and understanding can reveal solutions that others miss. It also suggests that what seems hopeless may simply need the right person to come along. ✓

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a detail given about the clockmaker's appearance?
options: Wild white hair
Fingers stained with oil
Thick magnifying spectacles
A long brown apron
Bright eyes like polished pennies
questions.correctAnswer: A long brown apron
questions.explanation: The passage describes the clockmaker's wild white hair, oil-stained fingers, thick magnifying spectacles, and bright eyes 'like polished pennies'. A long brown apron is never mentioned. ✓

questions.question: Why could Ellie and Sam see behind the waterfall for the first time?
options: The waterfall had been switched off
A drought had reduced the waterfall to a thin trickle
Someone had moved the rocks aside
They had brought special equipment
The waterfall had frozen solid
questions.correctAnswer: A drought had reduced the waterfall to a thin trickle
questions.explanation: The passage explains that August was extremely dry — 'the driest month anyone could remember' — and the river had 'shrunk to a trickle'. The waterfall became a 'thin, whispering veil', allowing them to see the rock face and the hidden opening behind it for the first time. ✓

questions.question: Why did Ellie stop herself from touching the paintings?
options: She was afraid they were cursed
She knew that touching ancient artefacts could damage them
She didn't want to get paint on her hands
Sam told her not to touch them
She thought the paint was poisonous
questions.correctAnswer: She knew that touching ancient artefacts could damage them
questions.explanation: Ellie 'reached out, then stopped herself' because 'she knew you weren't supposed to touch things like this'. This implies she understands that ancient paintings and artefacts are fragile and can be damaged by touch — oils from skin can deteriorate the pigments. She photographs them instead, showing responsible behaviour. ✓

questions.question: What does the word 'ochre' refer to in this passage?
options: A type of stone used for building
A natural earth pigment used for painting
A kind of ancient tool
A type of animal skin
A mineral used for cooking
questions.correctAnswer: A natural earth pigment used for painting
questions.explanation: Ochre is a natural earth pigment — a coloured clay — that was used by ancient people to create paint. The passage describes paintings 'drawn in red and brown ochre', telling us it is the colouring material used to make the cave art. ✓

questions.question: What is most likely to happen after this passage ends?
options: Ellie and Sam will keep the cave a secret forever
They will paint their own pictures on the walls
They will tell an adult or expert about the cave paintings
They will try to remove the paintings from the wall
They will go swimming in the pool instead
questions.correctAnswer: They will tell an adult or expert about the cave paintings
questions.explanation: Ellie says 'We need to tell someone — this could be really important' and Sam nods in agreement. She has already taken careful photographs as evidence. The most logical next step is that they will report their discovery to an adult — likely their parents, a teacher, or eventually archaeologists who can study the paintings properly. ✓

questions.question: The author describes the waterfall as a 'thundering curtain of water' that became a 'thin, whispering veil'. What is the effect of this contrast?
options: It shows that curtains and veils are similar
It dramatically shows how much the waterfall changed during the drought
It proves that waterfalls are always noisy
It describes two different waterfalls
It shows that Ellie prefers quiet places
questions.correctAnswer: It dramatically shows how much the waterfall changed during the drought
questions.explanation: The author uses contrasting metaphors — 'thundering curtain' versus 'thin, whispering veil' — to vividly show the dramatic change caused by the drought. The shift from powerful, loud, and thick to delicate, quiet, and transparent helps the reader understand just how extreme the dry weather was and why the hidden cave entrance became visible for the first time. ✓

questions.question: Why does the author end the passage with Sam thinking about the ancient child's handprint?
options: To make Sam seem cleverer than Ellie
To create a personal connection between the present and the distant past
To explain how handprints are made
To suggest Sam wants to be a painter
To show that the cave is haunted
questions.correctAnswer: To create a personal connection between the present and the distant past
questions.explanation: By ending with Sam realising a child his own age once stood in the same spot, the author creates a powerful emotional connection across thousands of years. It makes the ancient past feel real and personal, helping the reader feel the same sense of wonder — that history is not just facts in books, but real people who once lived and breathed. ✓

questions.question: Who wrote the old guidebook that Aisha found?
options: Aisha's father
A fell-walker named Margaret Booth
The village librarian
A mountain rescue volunteer
A local shepherd
questions.correctAnswer: A fell-walker named Margaret Booth
questions.explanation: The passage states 'An old guidebook from 1963, written by a fell-walker named Margaret Booth'. This is directly stated in the text. ✓

questions.question: What do the letters 'M.B.' on the carved stone most likely stand for?
options: Mountain Base
Margaret Booth
Map Below
Mile Boundary
Moss Bank
questions.correctAnswer: Margaret Booth
questions.explanation: The carved stone was described in Margaret Booth's guidebook, and the initials M.B. match her name perfectly. She was the fell-walker who discovered and documented the hidden path. It makes sense that she would have carved her initials on the marker stone to help future walkers find the route. ✓

questions.question: What does the word 'loomed' tell us about how the mountain appeared?
options: It was small and easy to miss
It towered over the valley in a threatening or impressive way
It was brightly lit by sunshine
It was hidden behind trees
It was crumbling and falling apart
questions.correctAnswer: It towered over the valley in a threatening or impressive way
questions.explanation: The word 'loomed' means to appear large, imposing, and slightly threatening. It tells us the mountain dominated the view of the valley and felt intimidating — which is reinforced by the comparison to 'a dark throne' and the fact that nobody had climbed it for twenty years. ✓

questions.question: Based on the ending, what do you think Aisha and her father will do next?
options: Turn around and go home immediately
Follow the hidden path up the mountain
Report the carved stone to the police
Go back to the library to read more
Wait for other walkers to arrive
questions.correctAnswer: Follow the hidden path up the mountain
questions.explanation: The father says 'After you', inviting Aisha to lead the way up the path. They have already found the carved stone (confirming the guidebook is accurate), brought proper gear, and made a deal about safety. Everything points to them following Margaret Booth's hidden path up the eastern side of the mountain. ✓

questions.question: Which of the following items did Aisha's father NOT mention as something they should take?
options: Waterproofs
A compass
A first-aid kit
A charged phone
Enough food for the day
questions.correctAnswer: A first-aid kit
questions.explanation: Aisha's father listed four items: 'Waterproofs, a compass, a charged phone, and enough food for the day.' A first-aid kit was never mentioned. This is a negative retrieval question — you must identify the item that is NOT in the text. ✓

questions.question: The author writes that Aisha could see her father 'weighing it up — the sensible parent part of his brain fighting against the adventurous part.' What technique is this and what does it reveal?
options: Onomatopoeia — it creates a sound effect of thinking
Personification — it gives his brain human qualities of conflict to show his inner struggle
Alliteration — it repeats the 'p' sound to create rhythm
A flashback — it shows a memory from his childhood
Hyperbole — it exaggerates how long he took to decide
questions.correctAnswer: Personification — it gives his brain human qualities of conflict to show his inner struggle
questions.explanation: This is personification — the author gives parts of the father's brain human qualities ('fighting against' each other). It vividly reveals his inner conflict between being a responsible, cautious parent and wanting to join the adventure himself. The technique helps the reader understand his dilemma in a clear, relatable way. ✓

questions.question: What caused the gap in the wall that allowed Lily to enter the garden?
options: Lily broke through the wall herself
Builders demolished a section
A fierce storm caused part of the wall to collapse
Mr Patterson knocked the stones down
The ivy pulled the wall apart over time
questions.correctAnswer: A fierce storm caused part of the wall to collapse
questions.explanation: The passage states that 'after a night of fierce wind and driving rain, Lily noticed something had changed. A section of the old wall had collapsed.' The storm caused the collapse, creating the gap through which Lily could enter. ✓

questions.question: What clues in the passage suggest this garden is not an ordinary garden?
options: It has a stone wall and ivy around it
There is a path leading to the fountain
Fruit grows in late October, water flows upward, and a tree has silver leaves
The roses are red and there is a robin
It has neat rows of trees
questions.correctAnswer: Fruit grows in late October, water flows upward, and a tree has silver leaves
questions.explanation: Several details suggest something magical: fruit trees are laden with apples and pears 'despite it being nearly November' (out of season), roses bloom with 'impossibly large' petals, the fountain water flows upward before falling, the garden seems 'far larger than seemed possible', and a tree has silver leaves. These are all impossible in an ordinary garden. ✓

questions.question: What does the word 'cascading' mean in the phrase 'cascading down into a pool'?
options: Dripping slowly
Pouring or flowing downward in a rush
Freezing into ice
Disappearing completely
Bubbling gently
questions.correctAnswer: Pouring or flowing downward in a rush
questions.explanation: The word 'cascading' means flowing or pouring downward rapidly, like a waterfall. In this context, the fountain water first flows impossibly upward, then cascades — rushes and tumbles — back down into the pool below. ✓

questions.question: The robin is described as watching Lily 'as though deciding whether she was welcome'. What technique is this and what mood does it create?
options: Alliteration — it creates a musical rhythm
Personification — it gives the robin human judgement, creating a sense of mystery and guardianship
A simile — it compares the robin to a person
Repetition — it echoes an earlier phrase
Onomatopoeia — it imitates the robin's song
questions.correctAnswer: Personification — it gives the robin human judgement, creating a sense of mystery and guardianship
questions.explanation: This is personification — the robin is given the human ability to make a decision about whether Lily is welcome. This creates a mysterious, almost magical mood, suggesting the garden has its own rules and guardians. The robin seems to be assessing Lily, as though the garden itself is watching her. ✓

questions.question: Why does the author include Mr Patterson's earlier claim about seeing silver leaves?
options: To make Mr Patterson seem silly and forgetful
To provide foreshadowing — preparing the reader for the magical discovery Lily will make
To explain why the wall was built
To show that Lily does not trust her neighbours
To describe the type of trees that grow locally
questions.correctAnswer: To provide foreshadowing — preparing the reader for the magical discovery Lily will make
questions.explanation: Mr Patterson's claim about silver leaves is foreshadowing — a technique where the author plants a hint about something that will happen later. When Lily initially dismisses the idea ('Trees don't have silver leaves'), the reader does too. But when she finds the tree exactly as described, it creates a satisfying sense of wonder and confirms that the garden truly is magical. ✓

questions.question: Which genre best describes this passage?
options: Science fiction set in outer space
Fantasy or magical realism
Historical non-fiction
A comedy story
A horror story
questions.correctAnswer: Fantasy or magical realism
questions.explanation: The passage is best described as fantasy or magical realism. It is set in a realistic, everyday location (a housing estate) but contains magical elements — fruit growing out of season, water flowing upward, a tree with silver leaves, and a garden bigger on the inside than the outside. These impossible details within an ordinary setting are hallmarks of the magical realism genre. ✓

questions.question: How far underground was the time capsule buried?
options: Half a metre
About a metre
Two metres
Three metres
Just below the surface
questions.correctAnswer: About a metre
questions.explanation: The passage states the tin box was 'buried about a metre underground'. This is directly stated in the first paragraph. ✓

questions.question: Why did the hall go quiet after Mr Okafor read the letter?
options: The children were bored and falling asleep
They were moved by the personal message from children who once sat where they sit now
Mr Okafor told them to be silent
They were confused by the old-fashioned words
The bell had rung for the next lesson
questions.correctAnswer: They were moved by the personal message from children who once sat where they sit now
questions.explanation: The silence shows the children were emotionally affected by the letter. The message from 1975 pupils — real children who attended the same school and asked to be remembered — created a powerful, moving moment. The connection across fifty years touched the listeners, as shown by Kai studying the photograph more carefully afterwards. ✓

questions.question: What does the word 'foundations' mean in 'digging foundations for the new school library'?
options: Decorations for the outside of a building
The underground base upon which a building is constructed
The roof and upper floors of a building
The furniture inside a room
The plans drawn by an architect
questions.correctAnswer: The underground base upon which a building is constructed
questions.explanation: Foundations are the solid underground structures — usually made of concrete — upon which a building is built. They provide a stable base. The builders were digging into the ground to create these foundations when they accidentally struck the buried time capsule. ✓

questions.question: What is the deeper message the author conveys through Mr Okafor's final words: 'They got remembered'?
options: That flying cars are not important
That being remembered by future generations is more valuable than technology
That head teachers always know the right thing to say
That time capsules should be buried in every school
That 1975 was a better time to be a child
questions.correctAnswer: That being remembered by future generations is more valuable than technology
questions.explanation: Mr Okafor's words carry the story's central theme: that human connection across time matters more than material progress. The 1975 children did not get the futuristic inventions they imagined, but their wish — 'Please remember us' — was granted. The author suggests that being remembered and making a lasting impression on others is one of the most meaningful things a person can achieve. ✓

questions.question: Which of the following was NOT listed as a favourite activity by the 1975 pupils?
options: Roller skating
Watching Crackerjack on the telly
Eating sherbet dip
Playing football in the park
None of the above — they were all mentioned
questions.correctAnswer: Playing football in the park
questions.explanation: The letter lists three favourite things: 'roller skating, watching Crackerjack on the telly, and eating sherbet dip.' Playing football in the park is never mentioned in the passage. ✓

questions.question: The author describes the boy in the photograph as looking 'exactly like someone you might sit next to in class today'. Why is this detail effective?
options: It proves the photograph is a fake
It shows that children have not changed much across generations, creating a bond between past and present
It means the boy is a relative of someone in Year Six
It suggests all school photographs look the same
It is included to make the reader laugh
questions.correctAnswer: It shows that children have not changed much across generations, creating a bond between past and present
questions.explanation: By describing the 1975 boy as looking like a modern classmate, the author bridges the fifty-year gap between the two groups of children. It makes the past feel close and real rather than distant and abstract. The detail creates empathy — the reader realises that the children who buried the capsule were just like children today, making their wish to be remembered all the more touching. ✓

questions.question: What time was it when Finn noticed Rosie had not come home?
options: Half past five
Six o'clock
Half past six
Half past seven
Eight o'clock
questions.correctAnswer: Half past seven
questions.explanation: The passage states 'now it was half past seven, the sky was turning violet, and her basket sat empty on the kitchen step.' The time is directly stated in the text. ✓

questions.question: Why does the author say 'He didn't decide to go. His feet simply started walking'?
options: Finn was sleepwalking
His love for his sister was so strong that going to find her felt automatic and instinctive
Someone pushed him forward
He was being controlled by the whispering sounds
He tripped and fell towards the wood
questions.correctAnswer: His love for his sister was so strong that going to find her felt automatic and instinctive
questions.explanation: The author shows that Finn's protective instinct for his sister was so powerful that it overcame his fear. He didn't consciously weigh up the danger — his body moved before his mind could talk him out of it. This reveals the strength of the bond between the siblings and Finn's courage. ✓

questions.question: What does the word 'dusk' mean in the phrase 'The wood was different at dusk'?
options: The middle of the night when it is completely dark
Early morning when the sun is rising
The time just before it gets fully dark in the evening
A bright, sunny afternoon
A foggy morning
questions.correctAnswer: The time just before it gets fully dark in the evening
questions.explanation: Dusk is the period of fading light in the evening, just after sunset and before full darkness. The passage shows this transition — the sky is 'turning violet' and the trees are becoming 'dark shapes'. It is the in-between time when daylight is disappearing but night has not fully arrived. ✓

questions.question: The author writes that the silence was 'heavy and watchful, as though the whole wood was holding its breath'. What two techniques are used here?
options: Alliteration and a simile
A metaphor and onomatopoeia
Personification and a simile, making the wood seem alive and aware
Repetition and rhyme
Hyperbole and a rhetorical question
questions.correctAnswer: Personification and a simile, making the wood seem alive and aware
questions.explanation: Two techniques work together here. Personification gives the wood human qualities — it is 'watchful' and 'holding its breath', as though it is a living thing that is aware of Finn. The phrase 'as though' introduces a simile, comparing the silence to a person holding their breath. Together, these techniques create an eerie, tense atmosphere where the forest itself seems conscious and alert. ✓

questions.question: Why does the author end the story with both Finn and Rosie claiming they were not scared, when both clearly were?
options: To show they are both dishonest children
To demonstrate how siblings protect each other's feelings through shared understanding
To prove that the wood is not really frightening
To suggest they will go back into the wood tomorrow
To show that children never feel fear
questions.correctAnswer: To demonstrate how siblings protect each other's feelings through shared understanding
questions.explanation: Both children were clearly frightened — Rosie's tears and Finn's tightening throat prove it. But by each claiming they were not scared, and Finn accepting Rosie's claim without question, the author shows the tender, protective bond between siblings. They are shielding each other's pride. Finn's 'Neither was I' is an act of kindness, not a lie — it tells Rosie she has nothing to be embarrassed about. ✓

questions.question: What do you think will happen when Finn and Rosie arrive home?
options: Their parents will be angry and punish them both
Their parents will not have noticed they were gone
Their parents will be hugely relieved, and Finn may be praised for finding Rosie
They will decide to move house immediately
Their parents will send them back to search for the blackberries
questions.correctAnswer: Their parents will be hugely relieved, and Finn may be praised for finding Rosie
questions.explanation: Both parents were already actively searching — the mother phoning neighbours and the father checking lanes with a torch. They will be hugely relieved when both children return safely. Finn went into the wood alone, at dusk, breaking the village rule to rescue his sister — an act of bravery that his parents are likely to acknowledge, even if they also express concern about the danger. ✓

questions.question: On which day did the coding club meet?
options: Monday lunchtime
Tuesday after school
Wednesday lunchtime
Thursday lunchtime
Friday morning
questions.correctAnswer: Wednesday lunchtime
questions.explanation: The passage states 'The club met every Wednesday lunchtime in the ICT suite.' This information is directly stated in the text. ✓

questions.question: Why did the number of club members grow so quickly from three to eleven?
options: Mrs Okoro made it compulsory for Year 5
The headteacher offered house points for joining
Pupils were excited after seeing Zara's asteroid game
Parents wrote letters asking for more computing lessons
The ICT suite had brand new computers installed
questions.correctAnswer: Pupils were excited after seeing Zara's asteroid game
questions.explanation: The passage says 'Word had spread that Zara had built a game where a cartoon cat dodged falling asteroids, and everyone wanted to see it.' This implies that seeing what was possible in the club attracted more members. ✓

questions.question: What does the word 'tweaking' mean in the sentence 'Zara stayed behind after every session, tweaking her asteroid game'?
options: Deleting and starting again
Making small improvements and adjustments
Breaking something by accident
Copying someone else's work
Playing the game for fun
questions.correctAnswer: Making small improvements and adjustments
questions.explanation: In this context, 'tweaking' means making small adjustments and improvements. The passage goes on to list what Zara changed — adding levels, sound effects, and a high-score table — which are all refinements to her existing game. ✓

questions.question: Why does the author have Deepa win the competition instead of Zara?
options: To show that Zara was not very talented
To suggest that animations are always better than games
To show that success is not always about the main character, and quiet effort matters too
To prove that Mrs Okoro had a favourite pupil
To make the reader feel sorry for Zara
questions.correctAnswer: To show that success is not always about the main character, and quiet effort matters too
questions.explanation: The author deliberately has Deepa — described as 'a quiet girl' — win first place. This shows that success can come from unexpected places, and that Zara's journey was about discovering a passion for creating, not just winning. It adds depth to the story and avoids a predictable ending. ✓

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT mentioned as something Zara added to her asteroid game?
options: Levels
Sound effects
A high-score table
Multiplayer mode
All of the above were mentioned
questions.correctAnswer: Multiplayer mode
questions.explanation: The passage says Zara added 'levels, sound effects, and a high-score table.' Multiplayer mode is never mentioned in the passage. The other three features are all explicitly listed. ✓

questions.question: Why does Mrs Okoro mention the Wright brothers?
options: To teach the children about the history of flight
To encourage Marcus by showing that even famous achievements had small beginnings
To change the subject away from the competition
To explain how aeroplanes are built
To suggest the children should study science instead of computing
questions.correctAnswer: To encourage Marcus by showing that even famous achievements had small beginnings
questions.explanation: Mrs Okoro uses the Wright brothers as an analogy. Marcus was doubtful because they had only been coding for a month. By pointing out that the Wright brothers' first flight lasted just twelve seconds, she shows that great things start small. Her follow-up — 'Everyone starts somewhere' — reinforces this encouraging message. ✓

questions.question: Which house was Aiden in?
options: Oak
Elm
Ash
Birch
Willow
questions.correctAnswer: Birch
questions.explanation: The passage directly states 'Aiden was in Birch house, which hadn't won the Sports Day trophy since 2019.' ✓

questions.question: What was Callum's mistake in the race?
options: He tripped over a hurdle
He ran too fast at the start and tired himself out
He stopped to tie his shoelace
He ran in the wrong direction
He was distracted by the crowd
questions.correctAnswer: He ran too fast at the start and tired himself out
questions.explanation: The passage says 'Callum surged ahead immediately' and later 'Callum's pace had slowed — his earlier sprint had taken its toll.' This tells us he used too much energy at the start and didn't have enough left for the end of the race. ✓

questions.question: What does the word 'inevitable' mean in the phrase 'Elm's inevitable victory'?
options: Unlikely
Certain to happen
Unfair
Exciting
Delayed
questions.correctAnswer: Certain to happen
questions.explanation: The word 'inevitable' means something that is certain to happen and cannot be avoided. Callum was so confident that he believed Elm's victory was guaranteed — which makes Aiden's win even more surprising. ✓

questions.question: What is most likely to happen to Aiden in PE lessons after this event?
options: He will be asked to leave the school team
He will refuse to take part in any more races
He will be picked earlier for teams and gain more respect from classmates
He will stop reading books about the human body
He will become the PE teacher's assistant
questions.correctAnswer: He will be picked earlier for teams and gain more respect from classmates
questions.explanation: Before his win, Aiden was 'always the last to be picked for teams.' Now that he has shown he can beat the fastest boy in the year, classmates are likely to see him differently and pick him earlier. His unexpected talent for pacing has been revealed. ✓

questions.question: Where would you most likely read this passage?
options: In a science textbook about human bones
In a school newsletter reporting real results
In a children's story book or reading comprehension exercise
In a newspaper article about professional athletics
In a guide to organising sports events
questions.correctAnswer: In a children's story book or reading comprehension exercise
questions.explanation: This is a piece of realistic fiction — a made-up story set in a familiar school setting with believable characters. It has a narrative arc (underdog wins the race), dialogue, and descriptive language. You would most likely find it in a children's story book or reading comprehension exercise. ✓

questions.question: What effect does the phrase 'to absolute silence — and then an eruption of cheers' create?
options: It shows that nobody cared about the race
It creates a dramatic contrast between the shock and the celebration
It proves that the crowd was too far away to see
It suggests the weather was very windy
It shows that only a few people were watching
questions.correctAnswer: It creates a dramatic contrast between the shock and the celebration
questions.explanation: The author uses contrast (also called juxtaposition) between 'absolute silence' and 'an eruption of cheers.' The silence represents the crowd's shock that the smallest boy won, and the eruption shows their sudden, explosive joy. This contrast heightens the drama of the moment. ✓

questions.question: What is the large toothed wheel that the pedals turn called?
options: The sprocket
The chainring
The disc brake
The back axle
The hub
questions.correctAnswer: The chainring
questions.explanation: The passage states: 'When you push down on the pedals, you turn a large toothed wheel called the chainring.' This is directly stated in the text. ✓

questions.question: Why would a road cyclist NOT want to use wide, knobbly tyres?
options: They are too expensive for road cyclists
They are only available in one colour
They create more friction with the ground, which would slow the cyclist down
They are too heavy to carry on a road bike
They would damage the road surface
questions.correctAnswer: They create more friction with the ground, which would slow the cyclist down
questions.explanation: The passage explains that road bikes use thin, smooth tyres to reduce contact area and minimise friction for greater speed. Wide, knobbly tyres create more friction by gripping the surface more. A road cyclist needs speed on smooth tarmac, so extra grip would slow them down. ✓

questions.question: What does the word 'efficient' mean in the first sentence?
options: Very fast
Produces good results without wasting energy
Extremely heavy
Difficult to use
Recently invented
questions.correctAnswer: Produces good results without wasting energy
questions.explanation: In this context, 'efficient' means producing good results without wasting energy or effort. The passage explains that a bicycle converts leg energy into motion 'more effectively than almost any other form of transport,' which is what efficiency means. ✓

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT explained in the passage?
options: How the chain-and-gear system works
How brakes slow a bicycle down
How cyclists steer at high speeds
How bicycle gears are changed
How different tyres suit different surfaces
questions.correctAnswer: How bicycle gears are changed
questions.explanation: The passage explains the chain system, braking, steering by leaning, and tyre differences. However, it never explains how gears are changed — it only describes the chainring and sprocket relationship, not the mechanism for shifting between gears. ✓

questions.question: Where would you most likely read this passage?
options: In a novel about a cycling adventure
In a bicycle repair manual for mechanics
In an information book or encyclopaedia for children
In a newspaper report about a cycling race
In a poetry anthology
questions.correctAnswer: In an information book or encyclopaedia for children
questions.explanation: This passage is non-fiction and explains how a bicycle works using clear, factual language aimed at a young audience. It covers multiple aspects (gears, brakes, steering, tyres) in an accessible way. You would most likely find it in an information book or children's encyclopaedia. ✓

questions.question: Why does the author end with the sentence 'Its only power source is you'?
options: To warn readers that cycling is tiring
To criticise bicycles for not having engines
To end on a powerful, memorable note that highlights the simplicity and personal nature of cycling
To suggest that bicycles are old-fashioned technology
To encourage readers to buy an electric bicycle instead
questions.correctAnswer: To end on a powerful, memorable note that highlights the simplicity and personal nature of cycling
questions.explanation: The final sentence is short, direct, and personal — using 'you' to connect with the reader. After explaining the complex mechanics of a bicycle, the author ends by reminding us of the beautifully simple fact that it runs entirely on human power. This creates a memorable and satisfying conclusion. ✓

questions.question: How long did the children have to make their cakes?
options: Sixty minutes
Seventy-five minutes
Ninety minutes
Two hours
Forty-five minutes
questions.correctAnswer: Ninety minutes
questions.explanation: Their mum says: 'You've got ninety minutes to make the best cake you can.' This is directly stated in the passage. ✓
questions.passageTitle: The Great Bake Off at Home

questions.question: Why did Maisie choose a lemon drizzle cake instead of something more complicated?
options: It was the only recipe in the book
She didn't like chocolate
She had learned it from watching her nan and could almost make it from memory
Her mum told her to make it
It was the quickest cake to bake
questions.correctAnswer: She had learned it from watching her nan and could almost make it from memory
questions.explanation: The passage says Maisie 'had watched her nan make one dozens of times' and 'could almost do it from memory.' She chose it because it was familiar and she was confident she could make it well — a smart strategy compared to Joel's ambitious choice. ✓
questions.passageTitle: The Great Bake Off at Home

questions.question: What does 'methodically' mean in the sentence 'Maisie worked calmly and methodically'?
options: Quickly and carelessly
In a careful, organised, step-by-step way
With great difficulty
Slowly and reluctantly
While talking to someone else
questions.correctAnswer: In a careful, organised, step-by-step way
questions.explanation: The word 'methodically' means doing things in a careful, organised, step-by-step manner. This is supported by the details that follow — Maisie greased her tin, mixed her batter until smooth, and set the oven to exactly the right temperature. Everything was done carefully and in order. ✓
questions.passageTitle: The Great Bake Off at Home

questions.question: Why does the author compare the kitchen to a 'battlefield'?
options: To suggest the children were fighting with each other
To show that the kitchen was damaged beyond repair
To vividly convey the mess and chaos of two children baking at the same time
To explain that their mum was angry about the state of the kitchen
To show that baking is dangerous
questions.correctAnswer: To vividly convey the mess and chaos of two children baking at the same time
questions.explanation: Calling the kitchen a 'battlefield' is a metaphor. The author uses it to paint a vivid picture of the mess — flour everywhere, chocolate bubbling over, a burned finger. It conveys the chaos and energy of the scene in a humorous way, rather than suggesting any real fighting or danger. ✓
questions.passageTitle: The Great Bake Off at Home

questions.question: Based on Joel's reaction at the end, what is he most likely to do next time it rains?
options: Refuse to bake ever again
Ask to have another bake-off and try to improve
Complain that the competition was unfair
Watch television all day instead
Ask his mum to bake for him
questions.correctAnswer: Ask to have another bake-off and try to improve
questions.explanation: Joel's positive reaction — grinning, saying his cake was 'the best thing I've ever eaten', and asking for more — shows he genuinely enjoyed the experience despite losing. His earlier comment, 'I like a challenge,' suggests he has a determined personality. He is most likely to want to try again and do better next time. ✓
questions.passageTitle: The Great Bake Off at Home

questions.question: What is the main message the author wants the reader to take from this story?
options: Baking is too messy for children to do at home
Simple recipes are always better than complicated ones
Having fun and trying something new matters more than winning
Children should not be allowed to use the oven
Rainy days are boring and there is nothing to do
questions.correctAnswer: Having fun and trying something new matters more than winning
questions.explanation: Although Maisie wins the competition, Joel is perfectly happy with his messy but delicious cake. The story shows that the real value was in the fun of trying something new together. Joel's enthusiasm despite 'losing' — and his genuine pride in his creation — carries the message that the experience matters more than the result. ✓
questions.passageTitle: The Great Bake Off at Home

questions.question: What percentage of the playground waste was packaging that could have been recycled?
options: Twenty-three per cent
Fifty per cent
Sixty-two per cent
Eighty-five per cent
Fifteen per cent
questions.correctAnswer: Sixty-two per cent
questions.explanation: The passage states: 'sixty-two per cent of the rubbish was packaging that could have been recycled.' This fact is directly stated in the text as part of their research findings. ✓

questions.question: How do we know that Mr Briggs was unhappy about the litter problem before the project started?
options: He wrote a letter to the headteacher
He shouted at the children for dropping rubbish
The passage says he never complained but 'everyone could see he was fed up'
He refused to pick up the litter
He threatened to leave the school
questions.correctAnswer: The passage says he never complained but 'everyone could see he was fed up'
questions.explanation: The passage tells us 'He never complained, but everyone could see he was fed up.' This is an inference clue — Mr Briggs didn't say anything directly, but his feelings were visible to others. The detail that he spent a whole hour daily cleaning up also suggests frustration. ✓

questions.question: What does the word 'startling' mean in the phrase 'The results were startling'?
options: Boring and expected
Confusing and unclear
Surprising and shocking
Disappointing and sad
Amusing and funny
questions.correctAnswer: Surprising and shocking
questions.explanation: The word 'startling' means very surprising or shocking. The results were startling because such a high percentage of waste (eighty-five per cent) could have been recycled or composted — far more than anyone expected. ✓

questions.question: Why does the author include the detail about Mr Briggs planting a wildflower garden?
options: To show that Mr Briggs has become a professional gardener
To suggest the school now has a garden instead of a playground
To show a positive knock-on effect — reducing waste freed up time for something beautiful
To explain that the school has too much spare land
To suggest that recycling is no longer needed
questions.correctAnswer: To show a positive knock-on effect — reducing waste freed up time for something beautiful
questions.explanation: The wildflower garden detail shows a ripple effect of the recycling project. Because Mr Briggs no longer spends an hour picking up litter, he can use that time to create something positive and beautiful for the school. It reinforces the message that small changes can have wider benefits. ✓

questions.question: Which of the following was NOT one of the three changes the Eco Champions introduced?
options: Colour-coded recycling stations
A poster competition
A weekly league table for classes
A ban on bringing crisps to school
All of the above were introduced
questions.correctAnswer: A ban on bringing crisps to school
questions.explanation: The passage lists three changes: recycling stations, a poster competition, and a weekly league table. A ban on bringing crisps to school is never mentioned. The Eco Champions focused on better sorting and motivation, not on banning any foods. ✓

questions.question: Why does the author use specific numbers and percentages throughout this passage?
options: To make the passage longer and more difficult to read
To confuse the reader with too much information
To make the facts convincing and show the measurable impact of the project
To test whether the reader is good at maths
To show that Miss Sharma teaches mathematics
questions.correctAnswer: To make the facts convincing and show the measurable impact of the project
questions.explanation: The author includes precise figures (sixty-two per cent, forty per cent, fifty-eight per cent, fifteen minutes versus an hour) to make the project's impact feel real and measurable. Specific numbers are more convincing than vague statements like 'it got a lot better,' and they help the reader understand the scale of the achievement. ✓

questions.question: How many hours had Ravi spent on his tablet that Sunday?
options: One hour
Two hours
Three hours
Four hours
Five hours
questions.correctAnswer: Three hours
questions.explanation: Ravi's dad says: 'You've been on that tablet for three hours today.' This is directly stated in the passage. ✓

questions.question: Why does the author mention that Ravi's mum had been scrolling through a news app for forty minutes?
options: To show that the mum is a journalist
To highlight that adults can also spend too much time on screens, adding irony to her advice
To explain what news apps are used for
To prove that the mum was ignoring Ravi deliberately
To show that the family's Wi-Fi connection was working
questions.correctAnswer: To highlight that adults can also spend too much time on screens, adding irony to her advice
questions.explanation: There is a gentle irony in the mum criticising Ravi's screen time while she herself has been on her phone for forty minutes. The author includes this detail to show that screen time is a challenge for the whole family, not just children, and that the mum's advice — though valid — comes with a touch of hypocrisy. ✓

questions.question: What does the word 'protested' mean in the sentence 'Ravi protested'?
options: Agreed quietly
Objected and argued back
Whispered nervously
Asked politely
Shouted angrily
questions.correctAnswer: Objected and argued back
questions.explanation: The word 'protested' means to object or argue back against something you disagree with. Ravi was defending himself by explaining that his screen time had been educational, not just entertainment. It implies disagreement but not necessarily anger. ✓

questions.question: What is Omar most likely to do after hearing Ravi's advice?
options: Throw his tablet in the bin
Ignore Ravi completely because he doesn't own a tablet
Think about his own screen habits and perhaps try a similar approach
Tell Ravi that screens are always bad
Report Ravi to a teacher for using too much screen time
questions.correctAnswer: Think about his own screen habits and perhaps try a similar approach
questions.explanation: Ravi's conclusion is balanced and relatable — he is not saying screens are bad, just that they shouldn't 'take over everything else.' This is the kind of sensible, personal insight that would make a friend reflect on their own habits. Omar is most likely to consider trying something similar. ✓

questions.question: Which of the following was NOT listed as an off-screen activity Ravi could do in the Screen Time Swap?
options: Reading
Going outside
Helping with cooking
Drawing or painting
Practising his guitar
questions.correctAnswer: Drawing or painting
questions.explanation: The passage lists the off-screen swap activities as: reading, going outside, helping with cooking, playing a board game, or practising his guitar. Drawing or painting is never mentioned. ✓

questions.question: What is the author's overall viewpoint on screen time in this passage?
options: Screens are dangerous and should be banned for children
Screen time is always educational and parents worry too much
A balanced approach works best — screens are useful but should not replace other activities
Children should only use screens at school, never at home
Parents should not set any rules about screen time
questions.correctAnswer: A balanced approach works best — screens are useful but should not replace other activities
questions.explanation: The passage presents a balanced viewpoint. Ravi's screen use includes educational content, and the solution is not to ban screens but to balance them with off-screen activities. Ravi's final comment — 'It's not really about screens being bad... it's about making sure they don't take over everything else' — sums up the author's balanced message. ✓

questions.question: In which city was Alexander Graham Bell born?
options: London
New York
Edinburgh
Dublin
Boston
questions.correctAnswer: Edinburgh
questions.explanation: The passage states: 'Bell was born in Edinburgh, Scotland, in 1847.' This is directly stated in the text. ✓

questions.question: Why does the author say Bell's interest in sound was 'perhaps no surprise'?
options: Because Bell had always loved music
Because his family background was deeply connected to sound, speech, and deafness
Because all Scottish people were interested in science
Because telephones already existed when Bell was young
Because Bell's school taught science as the main subject
questions.correctAnswer: Because his family background was deeply connected to sound, speech, and deafness
questions.explanation: The author explains that Bell's mother was nearly deaf and his father was a speech teacher who worked with deaf pupils. Growing up in a household focused on sound and communication made it natural — 'perhaps no surprise' — that Bell would become interested in transmitting speech. ✓

questions.question: What does the word 'controversial' mean in the phrase 'This decision remained controversial for over a century'?
options: Universally celebrated
Causing disagreement and debate
Quickly forgotten
Extremely expensive
Completely fair
questions.correctAnswer: Causing disagreement and debate
questions.explanation: The word 'controversial' means causing disagreement, debate, or argument. The patent decision was controversial because other inventors, particularly Meucci and Gray, were working on similar devices, and many people believed Bell was not the only — or even the first — inventor of the telephone. ✓

questions.question: Where would you most likely read this passage?
options: In a telephone repair manual
In a collection of fairy tales
In a history or information book for children
In a personal diary
In a recipe book
questions.correctAnswer: In a history or information book for children
questions.explanation: This passage is non-fiction and provides historical facts about the invention of the telephone, including dates, names, and how the device worked. It is written in an accessible, engaging style for a young audience. You would most likely find it in a history or information book for children. ✓

questions.question: What is the effect of the author's comment that Bell 'refused to have a telephone in his study'?
options: It shows that Bell regretted inventing the telephone
It proves that early telephones did not work properly
It creates humour and irony — the inventor was annoyed by his own invention
It suggests that Bell was too poor to afford a telephone
It shows that telephones were banned in homes at that time
questions.correctAnswer: It creates humour and irony — the inventor was annoyed by his own invention
questions.explanation: The author ends with a humorous, ironic detail: the man who invented the telephone found it too distracting to have one in his own study. This creates a witty connection to modern life — as the author notes, anyone who has been interrupted by a ringing phone can relate. The irony makes the ending memorable and entertaining. ✓

questions.question: Why does the author begin the passage by describing modern smartphones before going back to 1876?
options: Because the author forgot to organise the passage properly
To explain how to use a smartphone
To create a contrast between today's technology and Bell's simple invention, making the history feel relevant
To advertise modern telephones
To suggest that Bell's invention was not important
questions.correctAnswer: To create a contrast between today's technology and Bell's simple invention, making the history feel relevant
questions.explanation: By starting with something familiar — the phone in your pocket — and then jumping back to 1876, the author creates a powerful contrast. It helps the reader appreciate how far technology has come and makes the historical content feel relevant and connected to their own life. This technique hooks the reader's interest from the very first line. ✓

questions.question: Where did the family go camping?
options: The Lake District
The Scottish Highlands
The New Forest
Dartmoor
The Peak District
questions.correctAnswer: The New Forest
questions.explanation: The passage states: 'this year's holiday would be a week of camping in the New Forest.' This is directly stated in the first paragraph. ✓

questions.question: Why did Dad struggle to put up the tent?
options: The tent was broken and had missing parts
He tried to do it without reading the instructions first
The ground was too hard to hammer pegs into
Margaret was distracting him with stories
Alfie kept pulling the tent down as a joke
questions.correctAnswer: He tried to do it without reading the instructions first
questions.explanation: The passage says Dad 'was confident he knew what he was doing' after watching a video, but after forty-five minutes he was 'reading the instructions he should have read from the start.' This tells us his mistake was trying to put up the tent without reading the instructions properly first. ✓

questions.question: What does the word 'communal' mean in the phrase 'a communal fire pit'?
options: Very large
Shared by everyone on the campsite
Made of stone
Only for adults
Hidden from view
questions.correctAnswer: Shared by everyone on the campsite
questions.explanation: The word 'communal' means shared by a group of people. The passage says it was a fire pit 'where families gathered in the evenings,' confirming that it was a shared space used by everyone at the campsite, not private to one family. ✓

questions.question: Which of the following did Lily NOT do during the camping trip?
options: Toast marshmallows
Paddle in the stream
Spot a kingfisher
Go horse riding on the wild ponies
Listen to Margaret's stories
questions.correctAnswer: Go horse riding on the wild ponies
questions.explanation: The passage mentions Lily toasting marshmallows, paddling in the stream, spotting a kingfisher, and listening to Margaret's stories. Although they saw wild ponies on the Wednesday walk, there is no mention of anyone riding them. The ponies were simply grazing beside the path. ✓

questions.question: What does the description 'a flash of electric blue that appeared and vanished in a heartbeat' help the reader to understand about the kingfisher?
options: That the kingfisher was very large
That it moved incredibly fast and its bright colour made a vivid, fleeting impression
That Lily was frightened by the bird
That the stream was very deep and blue
That the kingfisher was injured
questions.correctAnswer: That it moved incredibly fast and its bright colour made a vivid, fleeting impression
questions.explanation: This descriptive phrase uses vivid imagery to capture the kingfisher's speed and striking colour. 'A flash of electric blue' describes the intense colour, while 'appeared and vanished in a heartbeat' conveys how quickly the bird moved. Together, they create a sense of a brief but magical moment. ✓

questions.question: Why does the author include the detail that Dad folded the tent 'perfectly this time'?
options: To show that Dad had bought a new, easier tent
To suggest that Margaret had given him a different tent
To show Dad's growth — he learned from his earlier mistake and improved
To prove that folding a tent is easier than putting one up
To explain that the tent was too small
questions.correctAnswer: To show Dad's growth — he learned from his earlier mistake and improved
questions.explanation: At the start, Dad struggled with the tent and needed Margaret's help. The detail that he folded it 'perfectly this time' shows he has learned and improved over the week. It mirrors Lily's own journey from reluctance to enthusiasm — both father and daughter grew through the experience. ✓

questions.question: If the family goes camping again next year, how will Lily most likely behave differently?
options: She will refuse to go and stay at home
She will be excited from the start instead of dreading it
She will insist on staying in a hotel instead
She will bring her own separate tent
She will complain about the insects the whole time
questions.correctAnswer: She will be excited from the start instead of dreading it
questions.explanation: At the start, Lily's 'heart sank' at the idea of camping. By the end, she is sad to leave and asks to come for two weeks. Her attitude has completely changed. Next year, she would approach the trip with enthusiasm rather than dread, having experienced how enjoyable camping can be. ✓

questions.question: This passage is best described as which type of writing?
options: A persuasive text arguing that everyone should go camping
A set of instructions for how to put up a tent
A realistic fiction story about a family experience
A formal report on New Forest wildlife
An autobiography by a famous explorer
questions.correctAnswer: A realistic fiction story about a family experience
questions.explanation: This is realistic fiction — a made-up story set in a real, recognisable setting (the New Forest) with believable characters and events. It has a narrative arc (Lily goes from reluctance to enthusiasm), dialogue, descriptive language, and character development. It is not a factual report, a set of instructions, or a persuasive text. ✓

questions.question: In the sentence 'The wind howled fiercely around the tower,' what type of word is 'fiercely'?
options: A noun (naming word)
A verb (doing word)
An adjective (describing word)
An adverb (tells us how something is done)
A preposition (position word)
questions.correctAnswer: An adverb (tells us how something is done)
questions.explanation: 'Fiercely' is an adverb — it tells us HOW the wind howled. Most adverbs end in '-ly' and answer the question 'how?'. If you can ask 'how did it happen?' and the word answers that, it's an adverb! ✓

questions.question: In the sentence 'The ancient display cases were covered in dust,' what type of word is 'ancient'?
options: A noun
A verb
An adjective
An adverb
A pronoun
questions.correctAnswer: An adjective
questions.explanation: 'Ancient' is an adjective — it describes the display cases, telling us what they're like. Adjectives answer the question 'what kind of?' — what kind of display cases? Ancient ones! ✓

questions.question: In the sentence 'Hedgehogs sleep through the cold winter months,' what type of word is 'through'?
options: A noun
A verb
An adjective
An adverb
A preposition
questions.correctAnswer: A preposition
questions.explanation: 'Through' is a preposition — it shows the relationship between 'sleep' and 'winter months'. Prepositions are position/direction words like 'in', 'on', 'under', 'through', 'between'. ✓

questions.question: In the sentence 'Visitors can still see the steaming green water today,' what type of word is 'steaming'?
options: A noun
A verb
An adjective
An adverb
A conjunction
questions.correctAnswer: An adjective
questions.explanation: 'Steaming' is an adjective here — it describes the water (what kind of water? steaming water). Words ending in '-ing' can sometimes be verbs, but here it's describing a noun, so it's working as an adjective. ✓
questions.passageTitle: The Roman Baths of Aquae Sulis

questions.question: In the sentence 'The dark clouds gathered quickly above the village,' what type of word is 'gathered'?
options: A noun
A verb
An adjective
An adverb
A preposition
questions.correctAnswer: A verb
questions.explanation: 'Gathered' is a verb — it's the action word telling us what the clouds DID. Verbs are doing or being words. What did the clouds do? They gathered! ✓

questions.question: In the sentence 'She carefully placed the jar over the spider,' what type of word is 'carefully'?
options: A noun
A verb
An adjective
An adverb
A pronoun
questions.correctAnswer: An adverb
questions.explanation: 'Carefully' is an adverb — it tells us HOW she placed the jar. The '-ly' ending is a big clue! Adverbs usually describe verbs, telling us how, when, or where something happens. ✓

questions.question: In the sentence 'They build warm nests from dry leaves and grass,' what type of word is 'warm'?
options: A noun
A verb
An adjective
An adverb
A preposition
questions.correctAnswer: An adjective
questions.explanation: 'Warm' is an adjective — it describes the nests (what kind of nests? warm ones). Adjectives are describing words that tell us more about a noun. ✓

questions.question: In the sentence 'Tiny crabs scuttle between the rocks,' what type of word is 'between'?
options: A noun
A verb
An adjective
An adverb
A preposition
questions.correctAnswer: A preposition
questions.explanation: 'Between' is a preposition — it tells us WHERE the crabs scuttle (between the rocks). Prepositions show position or direction: in, on, under, between, through, beside. ✓

questions.question: In the sentence 'The busy workers collect nectar from hundreds of flowers,' what type of word is 'collect'?
options: A noun
A verb
An adjective
An adverb
A preposition
questions.correctAnswer: A verb
questions.explanation: 'Collect' is a verb — it's the action the workers do. What do the workers do? They collect! Verbs are the engine of every sentence — they tell us what's happening. ✓

questions.question: In the sentence 'Its enormous branches stretched across the entire meadow,' what type of word is 'enormous'?
options: A noun
A verb
An adjective
An adverb
A conjunction
questions.correctAnswer: An adjective
questions.explanation: 'Enormous' is an adjective — it tells us about the branches (how big? enormous!). Adjectives add detail to nouns, painting a picture in the reader's mind. ✓

questions.question: In the sentence 'The team drove rapidly towards the approaching storm,' what type of word is 'rapidly'?
options: A noun
A verb
An adjective
An adverb
A pronoun
questions.correctAnswer: An adverb
questions.explanation: 'Rapidly' is an adverb — it tells us HOW the team drove. That '-ly' ending is your best friend for spotting adverbs! ✓

questions.question: In the sentence 'The warriors sailed across the freezing sea,' what type of word is 'across'?
options: A noun
A verb
An adjective
An adverb
A preposition
questions.correctAnswer: A preposition
questions.explanation: 'Across' is a preposition — it shows the direction of the sailing (across the sea). Prepositions tell us about position and direction. ✓

questions.question: In the sentence 'She tended the wounded soldiers with great care,' what type of word is 'wounded'?
options: A noun
A verb
An adjective
An adverb
A preposition
questions.correctAnswer: An adjective
questions.explanation: 'Wounded' is an adjective here — it describes the soldiers (which soldiers? the wounded ones). Like 'steaming' and 'broken', words ending in '-ed' can work as adjectives when they describe a noun. ✓

questions.question: In the sentence 'Merchants traded silk, spices and precious stones,' what type of word is 'traded'?
options: A noun
A verb
An adjective
An adverb
A conjunction
questions.correctAnswer: A verb
questions.explanation: 'Traded' is a verb — it tells us what the merchants DID. It's in the past tense (they traded), which is how we know it's describing an action that already happened. ✓

questions.question: In the sentence 'Rich families ate lavishly while poor people often went hungry,' what type of word is 'while'?
options: A noun
A verb
An adjective
An adverb
A conjunction
questions.correctAnswer: A conjunction
questions.explanation: 'While' is a conjunction — it joins two parts of the sentence together and shows they're happening at the same time. Conjunctions are joining words: and, but, or, because, although, while. ✓

questions.question: In the sentence 'She crept silently between the towering shelves,' what type of word is 'silently'?
options: A noun
A verb
An adjective
An adverb
A preposition
questions.correctAnswer: An adverb
questions.explanation: 'Silently' is an adverb — it tells us HOW she crept. Spot the '-ly'! Adverbs usually modify verbs: she crept (how?) silently. ✓

questions.question: In the sentence 'Mia carefully unfolded the yellowed parchment,' what type of word is 'yellowed'?
options: A noun
A verb
An adjective
An adverb
A pronoun
questions.correctAnswer: An adjective
questions.explanation: 'Yellowed' is an adjective — it describes the parchment (what kind? yellowed/old). Past participles like 'yellowed', 'broken', and 'frozen' often work as adjectives. ✓

questions.question: In the sentence 'The creature roared with terrifying power,' what type of word is 'terrifying'?
options: A noun
A verb
An adjective
An adverb
A conjunction
questions.correctAnswer: An adjective
questions.explanation: 'Terrifying' is an adjective — it describes the power (what kind of power? terrifying power). Even though it looks like a verb form, here it's describing a noun. ✓

questions.question: In the sentence 'The cold waves crashed against the rocks,' what type of word is 'crashed'?
options: A noun (naming word)
A verb (doing word)
An adjective (describing word)
An adverb (how word)
A preposition (position word)
questions.correctAnswer: A verb (doing word)
questions.explanation: 'Crashed' is a verb — it's what the waves DID. Verbs are action words. What happened? The waves crashed! ✓

questions.question: In the sentence 'The delicate mechanism ticked softly inside the wooden case,' what type of word is 'softly'?
options: A noun
A verb
An adjective
An adverb
A preposition
questions.correctAnswer: An adverb
questions.explanation: 'Softly' is an adverb — it tells us HOW the mechanism ticked. That '-ly' is your clue! ✓

questions.question: In the sentence 'The children planted seeds in neat rows,' what type of word is 'in'?
options: A noun
A verb
An adjective
An adverb
A preposition
questions.correctAnswer: A preposition
questions.explanation: 'In' is a preposition — it tells us WHERE the seeds were planted (in the rows). Short words like 'in', 'on', 'at', 'by', 'to' are almost always prepositions. ✓

questions.question: In the sentence 'The postman delivers letters every morning,' what type of word is 'delivers'?
options: A noun (naming word)
A verb (doing word)
An adjective (describing word)
An adverb (how word)
A preposition (position word)
questions.correctAnswer: A verb (doing word)
questions.explanation: 'Delivers' is a verb — it's what the postman DOES. Verbs tell us the action in the sentence. ✓

questions.question: In the sentence 'She felt nervous and uncertain about her first day,' what type of word is 'uncertain'?
options: A noun
A verb
An adjective
An adverb
A conjunction
questions.correctAnswer: An adjective
questions.explanation: 'Uncertain' is an adjective — it describes how she felt. Adjectives don't just describe things you can see — they also describe feelings and emotions! ✓

questions.question: In the sentence 'He pedalled steadily up the steep hill,' what type of word is 'steadily'?
options: A noun
A verb
An adjective
An adverb
A preposition
questions.correctAnswer: An adverb
questions.explanation: 'Steadily' is an adverb — it tells us HOW he pedalled. The '-ly' ending is your biggest clue for spotting adverbs! ✓

questions.question: In the sentence 'The delicious aroma drifted through the kitchen,' what type of word is 'through'?
options: A noun
A verb
An adjective
An adverb
A preposition
questions.correctAnswer: A preposition
questions.explanation: 'Through' is a preposition — it shows the direction the aroma moved (through the kitchen). Prepositions tell us about position and movement. ✓

questions.question: In the sentence 'Bees are incredibly important for our environment,' what type of word is 'important'?
options: A noun (naming word)
A verb (doing word)
An adjective (describing word)
An adverb (how word)
A preposition (position word)
questions.correctAnswer: An adjective (describing word)
questions.explanation: 'Important' is an adjective — it describes the bees (bees are important). Adjectives tell us what something is like. ✓

questions.question: In the sentence 'Strange creatures lurk in the darkness below,' what type of word is 'below'?
options: A noun
A verb
An adjective
An adverb
A preposition
questions.correctAnswer: An adverb
questions.explanation: 'Below' is an adverb here — it tells us WHERE the darkness is. Some words like 'below', 'above', 'outside' can be either adverbs or prepositions depending on how they're used. Here it modifies 'darkness' by telling us its location. ✓

questions.question: In the sentence 'Lightning strikes are both dangerous and fascinating,' what type of word is 'and'?
options: A noun
A verb
An adjective
An adverb
A conjunction
questions.correctAnswer: A conjunction
questions.explanation: 'And' is a conjunction — it joins 'dangerous' and 'fascinating' together. Conjunctions are the glue words of English: and, but, or, so, because, although. ✓

questions.question: In the sentence 'Molten lava flows slowly down the mountainside,' what type of word is 'slowly'?
options: A noun
A verb
An adjective
An adverb
A preposition
questions.correctAnswer: An adverb
questions.explanation: 'Slowly' is an adverb — it tells us HOW the lava flows. Spot the '-ly'! ✓

questions.question: In the sentence 'These remarkable forests have survived for thousands of years,' what type of word is 'remarkable'?
options: A noun
A verb
An adjective
An adverb
A pronoun
questions.correctAnswer: An adjective
questions.explanation: 'Remarkable' is an adjective — it describes the forests. Adjectives add colour and detail to nouns, helping the reader picture exactly what the writer means. ✓

questions.question: In the sentence 'The stars twinkled brightly above the quiet countryside,' what type of word is 'above'?
options: A noun
A verb
An adjective
An adverb
A preposition
questions.correctAnswer: A preposition
questions.explanation: 'Above' is a preposition here — it tells us WHERE the stars twinkled (above the countryside). When 'above' is followed by a noun, it's a preposition showing position. ✓
questions.passageTitle: A Guide to the Night Sky

questions.question: In the sentence 'Volunteers worked tirelessly to create safe routes,' what type of word is 'tirelessly'?
options: A noun
A verb
An adjective
An adverb
A conjunction
questions.correctAnswer: An adverb
questions.explanation: 'Tirelessly' is an adverb — it describes HOW the volunteers worked. That '-ly' ending is your best clue for adverbs! ✓

questions.question: In the sentence 'The tiny raindrop fell from the grey cloud,' what type of word is 'fell'?
options: A noun (naming word)
A verb (doing word)
An adjective (describing word)
An adverb (how word)
A preposition (position word)
questions.correctAnswer: A verb (doing word)
questions.explanation: 'Fell' is a verb — it tells us what the raindrop DID. It's the past tense of 'fall'. ✓

questions.question: In the sentence 'The peregrine falcon dives at extraordinary speed,' what type of word is 'extraordinary'?
options: A noun
A verb
An adjective
An adverb
A preposition
questions.correctAnswer: An adjective
questions.explanation: 'Extraordinary' is an adjective — it describes the speed (what kind of speed? extraordinary speed!). Even long, impressive-sounding words can be adjectives! ✓

questions.question: In the sentence 'Archaeologists discovered the tomb beneath the sand,' what type of word is 'beneath'?
options: A noun
A verb
An adjective
An adverb
A preposition
questions.correctAnswer: A preposition
questions.explanation: 'Beneath' is a preposition — it tells us WHERE the tomb was (beneath the sand). 'Beneath', 'under', 'below' are all prepositions that show something is lower than something else. ✓

questions.question: Why does Tom climb the lighthouse steps 'two at a time'?
options: He wants to exercise
He is racing against Barnacle
He is in a hurry because he is worried about something
